and the Wolff clustering algorithm. They incorporate global updates which
generate new lattice configurations with little correlation to previous states,
even near the critical point. We look to accelerate the rate at which these
algorithm are capable of running by implementing and benchmarking a parallel
implementation of each algorithm designed to run on GPUs under NVIDIA's CUDA
framework. A 17 and 90 fold increase in the computational rate was respectively
experienced when measured against the equivalent algorithm implemented in
serial code.

Rotational light modulation in Kepler photometry of K - A stars is used to
estimate the absolute rotational shear. The rotation frequency spread in 2562
carefully selected stars with known rotation periods is measured using
time-frequency diagrams. The variation of rotational shear as a function of
effective temperature in restricted ranges of rotation period is determined.
The shear increases to a maximum in F stars, but decreases somewhat in the A
stars. Theoretical models reproduce the temperature variation quite well. The
dependence of rotation shear on rotation rate in restricted temperature ranges
is also determined. The dependence of the shear on the rotation rate is weak in
K and G stars, increases rapidly for F stars and is strongest in A stars. For
stars earlier than type K, a discrepancy exists between the predicted and
observed variation of shear with rotation rate. There is a strong increase in
the fraction of stars with zero frequency spread with increasing effective
temperature. The time-frequency diagrams for A stars are no different from
those in cool stars, further supporting the presence of spots in stars with
radiative envelopes.

In this paper we propose a LWR-like model for traffic flow on networks which
allows one to track several groups of drivers, each of them being characterized
only by their destination in the network. The path actually followed to reach
the destination is not assigned a priori, and can be chosen by the drivers
during the journey, taking decisions at junctions.
  The model is then used to describe three possible behaviors of drivers,
associated to three different ways to solve the route choice problem: 1.
Drivers ignore the presence of the other vehicles; 2. Drivers react to the
current distribution of traffic, but they do not forecast what will happen at
later times; 3. Drivers take into account the current and future distribution
of vehicles. Notice that, in the latter case, we enter the field of
differential games, and, if a solution exists, it likely represents a global
equilibrium among drivers.
  Numerical simulations highlight the differences between the three behaviors
and suggest the existence of multiple Wardrop equilibria.

Phenomenology similar to the nonreciprocal charge transport violating
Onsagers reciprocity relations can develop in directionally inhomogeneous
conducting films with nonuniform Hall coefficient along the current trajectory.
The effect is demonstrated in ferromagnetic CoPd films and analyzed in
comparison with the unidirectional magnetoresistance phenomena. We suggest to
use an engineered inhomogeneity for spintronics applications and present the
concept of mixed symmetry Hall devices in which transverse to current Hall
signal is measured in a longitudinal contacts arrangement. Magnetization
reversal and memory detection is demonstrated in the three terminal and the
partitioned normal metal-ferromagnet (NM - FM) device designs. Multi-bit memory
is realized in the partitioned FM-NM-FM structure. The relative amplitude of
the antisymmetric signal in the engineered ferromagnetic devices is few percent
which is 10 to 1000 times higher than in their unidirectional magnetoresistance
analogues.

This article aims at establishing new benchmark scenarios for Galactic
cosmic-ray propagation in the GV-TV rigidity range, based on fits to the AMS-02
B/C data with the USINE v3.5 propagation code. We employ a new fitting
procedure, cautiously taking into account data systematic error correlations in
different rigidity bins and considering Solar modulation potential and leading
nuclear cross-section as nuisance parameters. We delineate specific low,
intermediate, and high-rigidity ranges that can be related to both features in
the data and peculiar microphysics mechanisms resulting in spectral breaks. We
single out a scenario which yields excellent fits to the data and includes all
the presumably relevant complexity, the BIG model. This model has two limiting
regimes: (i) the SLIM model, a minimal diffusion-only setup, and (ii) the
QUAINT model, a convection-reacceleration model where transport is tuned by
non-relativistic effects. All models lead to robust predictions in the
high-energy regime ($\gtrsim10$GV), i.e. independent of the propagation
scenario: at $1\sigma$, the diffusion slope $\delta$ is $[0.43-0.53]$, whereas
$K_{10}$, the diffusion coefficient at 10GV, is $[0.26-0.36]$kpc$^2$Myr$^{-1}$;
we confirm the robustness of the high-energy break, with a typical value
$\Delta_h\sim 0.2$. We also find a hint for a similar (reversed) feature at low
rigidity around the B/C peak ($\sim 4$GV) which might be related to some
effective damping scale in the magnetic turbulence.

Understanding electrical transport in strange metals, including the seeming
universality of Planckian $T$-linear resistivity, remains a longstanding
challenge in condensed matter physics. We propose that local imaging
techniques, such as nitrogen vacancy center magnetometry, can locally identify
signatures of quantum critical response which are invisible in measurements of
a bulk electrical resistivity. As an illustrative example, we use a minimal
holographic model for a strange metal in two spatial dimensions to predict how
electrical current will flow in regimes dominated by quantum critical dynamics
on the Planckian length scale. We describe the crossover between quantum
critical transport and hydrodynamic transport (including Ohmic regimes), both
in charge neutral and finite density systems. We compare our holographic
predictions to experiments on charge neutral graphene, finding quantitative
agreement with available data; we suggest further experiments which may
determine the relevance of our framework to transport on Planckian scales in
this material. More broadly, we propose that locally imaged transport be used
to test the universality (or lack thereof) of microscopic dynamics in the
diverse set of quantum materials exhibiting $T$-linear resistivity.

The 3D Bondi-Metzner-Sachs (BMS$_3$) algebra that is the asymptotic symmetry
algebra at null infinity of the $1+2$D asymptotically flat space-time is
isomorphic to the $1+1$D Carrollian conformal algebra. Building on this
connection, various preexisting results in the BMS$_3$-invariant field theories
are reconsidered in light of a purely Carrollian perspective in this paper. In
direct analogy to the covariant transformation laws of the Lorentzian tensors,
the flat Carrollian multiplets are defined and their conformal transformation
properties are established. A first-principle derivation of the Ward identities
in a $1+1$D Carrollian conformal field theory (CCFT) is presented. This
derivation introduces the use of the complex contour-integrals (over the
space-variable) that provide a strong analytic handle to CCFT. The temporal
step-function factors appearing in these Ward identities enable the translation
of the operator product expansions (OPEs) into the language of the operator
commutation relations and vice versa, via a contour-integral prescription.
Motivated by the properties of these step-functions, the $i\epsilon$-forms of
the Ward identities and OPEs are proposed that permit for the hassle-free use
of the algebraic properties of the latter. Finally, utilizing the computational
techniques developed, it is shown that the modes of the quantum energy-momentum
tensor operator generate the centrally extended version of the
infinite-dimensional $1+1$D Carrollian conformal algebra.

The effective extraction of ranked disease-symptom relationships is a
critical component in various medical tasks, including computer-assisted
medical diagnosis or the discovery of unexpected associations between diseases.
While existing disease-symptom relationship extraction methods are used as the
foundation in the various medical tasks, no collection is available to
systematically evaluate the performance of such methods. In this paper, we
introduce the Disease-Symptom Relation collection (DSR-collection), created by
five fully trained physicians as expert annotators. We provide graded symptom
judgments for diseases by differentiating between "symptoms" and "primary
symptoms". Further, we provide several strong baselines, based on the methods
used in previous studies. The first method is based on word embeddings, and the
second on co-occurrences of keywords in medical articles. For the co-occurrence
method, we propose an adaption in which not only keywords are considered, but
also the full text of medical articles. The evaluation on the DSR-collection
shows the effectiveness of the proposed adaption in terms of nDCG, precision,
and recall.

We address the problem of few-shot classification where the goal is to learn
a classifier from a limited set of samples. While data-driven learning is shown
to be effective in various applications, learning from less data still remains
challenging. To address this challenge, existing approaches consider various
data augmentation techniques for increasing the number of training samples.
Pseudo-labeling is commonly used in a few-shot setup, where approximate labels
are estimated for a large set of unlabeled images. We propose DiffAlign which
focuses on generating images from class labels. Specifically, we leverage the
recent success of the generative models (e.g., DALL-E and diffusion models)
that can generate realistic images from texts. However, naive learning on
synthetic images is not adequate due to the domain gap between real and
synthetic images. Thus, we employ a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) loss to
align the synthetic images to the real images minimizing the domain gap. We
evaluate our method on the standard few-shot classification benchmarks:
CIFAR-FS, FC100, miniImageNet, tieredImageNet and a cross-domain few-shot
classification benchmark: miniImageNet to CUB. The proposed approach
significantly outperforms the stateof-the-art in both 5-shot and 1-shot setups
on these benchmarks. Our approach is also shown to be effective in the
zero-shot classification setup

The Strange Quark matter (SQM) hypothesis states that at extreme pressure and
density conditions a new ground state of matter would arise, in which half of
the \textit{down} quarks become strange quarks. If true, it would mean that at
least the core of neutron stars is made of SQM. In this hypothesis, SQM would
be released in the inter-stellar medium when two of these objects merge. It is
estimated that $10^{-2} M\odot$ of SQM would be released this way. This matter
will undergo a sequence of processes that should result in a fraction of the
released SQM becoming heavy nuclei through \textit{r-process}. In this work we
are interested in characterizing the fragmentation of SQM, with the novelty of
keeping track of the \textit{quark configuration} of the fragmented matter.
This is accomplished by developing a methodology to estimate the energy of each
fragment as the sum of its \textit{constituent quarks}, the Coulomb interaction
among the quarks and fragments' momenta. The determination of the fragmentation
output is crucial to fully characterize the subsequent nucleosynthesis.

We report the direct detection of the kinetic Sunyaev-Zel'dovich (kSZ) effect
in galaxy clusters with a 3.5 sigma significance level. The measurement was
performed by stacking the Planck map at 217 GHz at the positions of galaxy
clusters from the Wen-Han-Liu (WHL) catalog. To avoid the cancelation of
positive and negative kSZ signals, we used the large-scale distribution of the
Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) galaxies to estimate the peculiar velocities of
the galaxy clusters along the line of sight and incorporated the sign in the
velocity-weighted stacking of the kSZ signals. Using this technique, we were
able to measure the kSZ signal around galaxy clusters beyond 3R500. Assuming a
standard beta-model, we also found that the gas fraction within R500 is
fgas,500 = 0.12 +- 0.04 for the clusters with the mass of M500 ~ 1e14 Msun/h.
We compared this result to predictions from the Magneticum cosmological
hydrodynamic simulations as well as other kSZ and X-ray measurements, most of
which show a lower gas fraction than the universal baryon fraction for the same
mass of clusters. Our value is statistically consistent with results from the
measurements and simulations and also with the universal value within our
measurement uncertainty.

Embedding-based neural topic models could explicitly represent words and
topics by embedding them to a homogeneous feature space, which shows higher
interpretability. However, there are no explicit constraints for the training
of embeddings, leading to a larger optimization space. Also, a clear
description of the changes in embeddings and the impact on model performance is
still lacking. In this paper, we propose an embedding regularized neural topic
model, which applies the specially designed training constraints on word
embedding and topic embedding to reduce the optimization space of parameters.
To reveal the changes and roles of embeddings, we introduce \textbf{uniformity}
into the embedding-based neural topic model as the evaluation metric of
embedding space. On this basis, we describe how embeddings tend to change
during training via the changes in the uniformity of embeddings. Furthermore,
we demonstrate the impact of changes in embeddings in embedding-based neural
topic models through ablation studies. The results of experiments on two
mainstream datasets indicate that our model significantly outperforms baseline
models in terms of the harmony between topic quality and document modeling.
This work is the first attempt to exploit uniformity to explore changes in
embeddings of embedding-based neural topic models and their impact on model
performance to the best of our knowledge.

The growing deployment of drones in a myriad of applications relies on
seamless and reliable wireless connectivity for safe control and operation of
drones. Cellular technology is a key enabler for providing essential wireless
services to flying drones in the sky. Existing cellular networks targeting
terrestrial usage can support the initial deployment of low-altitude drone
users, but there are challenges such as mobility support. In this paper, we
propose a novel handover framework for providing efficient mobility support and
reliable wireless connectivity to drones served by a terrestrial cellular
network. Using tools from deep reinforcement learning, we develop a deep
Q-learning algorithm to dynamically optimize handover decisions to ensure
robust connectivity for drone users. Simulation results show that the proposed
framework significantly reduces the number of handovers at the expense of a
small loss in signal strength relative to the baseline case where a drone
always connect to a base station that provides the strongest received signal
strength.

We examine the MHD instabilities arising in the radiation zone of a
differentially rotating star, in which a poloidal field of fossil origin is
sheared into a toroidal field. We focus on the non-axisymmetric instability
that affects the toroidal magnetic field in a rotating star, which was first
studied by Pitts and Tayler in the non-dissipative limit. According to Spruit,
it could also drive a dynamo. The Pitts & Tayler instability is manifestly
present in our simulations, with its conspicuous m=1 dependence in azimuth. But
its analytic treatment used so far is too simplified to be applied to the real
stellar situation. Although the instability generated field reaches an energy
comparable to that of the mean poloidal field, that field seems unaffected by
the instability: it undergoes Ohmic decline, and is neither eroded nor
regenerated by the instability. The toroidal field is produced by shearing the
poloidal field and it draws its energy from the differential rotation. The
small scale motions behave as Alfven waves; they cause negligible
eddy-diffusivity and contribute little to the net transport of angular
momentum. In our simulations we observe no sign of dynamo action, of either
mean field or fluctuation type, up to a magnetic Reynolds number of 10^5.
However the Pitts & Tayler instability is sustained as long as the differential
rotation acting on the poloidal field is able to generate a toroidal field of
sufficient strength.

We present the flux density measurements of the pulsars observed in the
Meterwavelength single-pulse polarimetric emission survey. The average flux
densities were estimated in 113 pulsars at two frequencies of 325 and 610 MHz
using interferometric imaging. The average profile and single pulse emission in
each pulsar were calibrated using the estimated flux density. We have used the
flux calibrated average profile to study the variation of the spectral index
across the emission beam in 21 pulsars where the core, inner cone and the outer
conal components could be clearly identified. The central core component showed
a steeper increase in emission at the lower frequency compared with conal
emission, with an average difference in spectral index
$\delta\alpha_{core-cone}\sim-0.7$ between the core and the conal components in
this frequency range. In contrast the inner conal components had positive
difference in their spectral index compared to the outer cones with average
difference $\delta\alpha_{in-out}\sim+0.3$. The variation in the spectral index
across the pulse window should provide valuable inputs for constraining the
radio emission processes. The single pulse emission showed the presence of
emission mode changing in 12 pulsars with 3 cases where the phenomenon is being
reported for the first time. In addition we have also detected enhanced
emission for short durations or flaring, in parts or across the entire emission
window in 14 pulsars. The sudden changes in the emission during mode changing
as well as these bursting states are unrelated to the emission mechanism and
suggest the presence of rapid and repetitive changes during the plasma
generation process.

A survey toward 674 Planck cold clumps of the Early Cold Core Catalogue (ECC)
in the J=1-0 transitions of $^{12}$CO, $^{13}$CO and C$^{18}$O has been carried
out using the PMO 13.7 m telescope. 673 clumps were detected with the $^{12}$CO
and $^{13}$CO, and 68% of the samples have C$^{18}$O emission. Additional
velocity components were also identified.A close consistency of the three line
peak velocities was revealed for the first time. Kinematic distances are given
out for all the velocity components and half of the clumps are located within
0.5 and 1.5 kpc. Excitation temperatures range from 4 to 27 K, slightly larger
than those of $T_d$. Line width analysis shows that the majority of ECC clumps
are low mass clumps. Column densities N$_{H_{2}}$ span from 10$^{20}$ to
4.5$\times10^{22}$ cm$^{-2}$ with an average value of
(4.4$\pm$3.6)$\times10^{21}$ cm$^{-2}$. N$_{H_{2}}$ cumulative fraction
distribution deviates from the lognormal distribution, which is attributed to
optical depth. The average abundance ratio of the $^{13}$CO to C$^{18}$O in
these clumps is 7.0$\pm$3.8, higher than the terrestrial value. Dust and gas
are well coupled in 95% of the clumps. Blue profile, red profile and line
asymmetry in total was found in less than 10% of the clumps, generally
indicating star formation is not developed yet. Ten clumps were mapped. Twelve
velocity components and 22 cores were obtained. Their morphologies include
extended diffuse, dense isolated, cometary and filament, of which the last is
the majority. 20 cores are starless.Only 7 cores seem to be in gravitationally
bound state. Planck cold clumps are the most quiescent among the samples of
weak-red IRAS, infrared dark clouds, UC H{\sc ii} region candidates, EGOs and
methanol maser sources, suggesting that Planck cold clumps have expanded the
horizon of cold Astronomy.

We study the influence of anomalous U(1) symmetries and their associated
D-terms on the vacuum structure of global field theories once they are coupled
to N=1 supergravity and in the context of string compactifications with moduli
stabilisation. In particular, we focus on a IIB string motivated construction
of the ISS scenario and examine the influence of one additional U(1) symmetry
on the vacuum structure. We point out that in the simplest one-Kahler modulus
compactification, the original ISS vacuum gets generically destabilised by a
runaway behaviour of the potential in the modulus direction. In more general
compactifications with several Kahler moduli, we find a novel realisation of
the LARGE volume scenario with D-term uplifting to de Sitter space and both
D-term and F-term supersymmetry breaking. The structure of soft supersymmetry
breaking terms is determined in the preferred scenario where the standard model
cycle is not stabilised non-perturbatively and found to be flavour universal.
Our scenario also provides a purely supersymmetric realisation of Kahler moduli
(blow-up and fibre) inflation, with similar observational properties as the
original proposals but without the need to include an extra (non-SUSY)
uplifting term.

Wavelet bases and frames consisting of band limited functions of nearly
exponential localization on Rd are a powerful tool in harmonic analysis by
making various spaces of functions and distributions more accessible for study
and utilization, and providing sparse representation of natural function spaces
(e.g. Besov spaces) on Rd. Such frames are also available on the sphere and in
more general homogeneous spaces, on the interval and ball. The purpose of this
article is to develop band limited well-localized frames in the general setting
of Dirichlet spaces with doubling measure and a local scale-invariant
Poincar\'e inequality which lead to heat kernels with small time Gaussian
bounds and H\"older continuity. As an application of this construction, band
limited frames are developed in the context of Lie groups or homogeneous spaces
with polynomial volume growth, complete Riemannian manifolds with Ricci
curvature bounded from below and satisfying the volume doubling property, and
other settings. The new frames are used for decomposition of Besov spaces in
this general setting.

Using data on 17 listed public banks from Russia over the period 2008 to
2016, we analyze whether international oil prices affect the bank stability in
an oil-dependent country. We posit that a decrease in international oil prices
has a negative long-run macroeconomic impact for an oil-exporting country,
which further deteriorates the bank financial stability. More specifically, a
decrease in international oil prices leads for an oil-exporting country as
Russia to a currency depreciation and to a deterioration of the fiscal stance.
In addition, given the positive correlation of oil and stock prices documented
by numerous previous studies, a decrease in international oil prices represents
a negative signal for the stock markets investors, negatively affecting banks'
share prices and thus, their capacity to generate sustainable earnings. In this
context, the bank financial stability can be menaced. With a focus on public
listed banks and using a Pool Mean Group (PMG) estimator, we show that an
increase in international oil prices and in the price to book value ratio has a
long-run positive effect on Russian public banks stability, and conversely.
While positive oil-price shocks contribute to bank stability in the long run,
an opposite effect is recorded for negative shocks. However, no significant
impact is documented in the short run. Our findings are robust to different
bank stability specifications, different samples and control variables.

Recent photometric and polarimetric observations of type Ia supernovae (SNe
Ia) show unusually low total-to-selective extinction ratio ($R_{V}<2$) and
wavelength of maximum polarization ($\lambda_{max}<0.4\mu m$) for several SNe
Ia, which indicates peculiar properties of interstellar (IS) dust in the SN
hosted galaxies and/or the presence of circumstellar (CS) dust. In this paper,
we use inversion technique to infer best-fit grain size distribution and
alignment function of interstellar grains along the lines of sight toward four
SNe Ia with anomalous extinction and polarization data (SNe 1986G, 2006X,
2008fp, and 2014J). We find that to reproduce low values of $R_{V}$, a
significant enhancement in the mass of small grains of radius $a< 0.1\mu m$ is
required. For SN 2014J, a simultaneous fit to observed extinction and
polarization data is unsuccessful if the entire data is attributed to IS dust
(model 1), but a good fit is obtained when accounting for the contribution of
CS dust (model 2). For SN 2008fp, our fitting results for model 1 show that, to
reproduce an extreme value of $\lambda_{\max}\sim 0.15\mu m$, very small
silicate grains must be aligned as efficiently as big grains. We suggest that
tiny grains in the intervening molecular cloud can be aligned efficiently by
radiative torques (RATs) from the SNe Ia. The resulting time dependence
polarization from this RAT alignment model can be tested by observing at
ultraviolet wavelengths. Our results are in favor of the existence of CS dust
in SN 2014J, but its presence in SN 2008fp remains uncertain.

We present the existence of the Kosterlitz-Thouless (KT) transition for
$n$-atic tangent-plane order on a deformable spherical surface and investigate
the development of quasi-long range $n$-atic order and the continuous shape
changes below the KT transition in the low temperature limit. The $n$-atic
order parameter $\psi= \exp[in\Theta]$ describes, respectively, vector,
nematic, and hexatic order for $n=1,2,$ and 6. We derive a Coulomb gas
Hamiltonian to describe it. We then convert it into the sine-Gordon Hamiltonian
and find a linear coupling between a scalar field and the Gaussian curvature.
After integrating over the shape fluctuations, we obtain the massive
sine-Gordon Hamiltonian, where the interaction between vortices is screened. We
find, for $n^{2}K_{n}/\kappa \ll 1/4$, there is an effective KT transition. In
the ordered phase, tangent-plane $n$-atic order expels the Gaussian curvature.
In addition, the total vorticity of orientational order on a surface of genus
zero is two. Thus, the ordered phase of an $n$-atic on such a surface will have
$2n$ vortices of strength $1/n$, $2n$ zeros in its order parameter, and a
nonspherical equilibrium shape. Our calculations are based on a
phenomenological model with a gauge-like coupling between $\psi$ and curvature
and close to the Abrikosov treatment of a type II superconductor.

A local resolution of the Problem of Time has recently been given, alongside
reformulation as a local theory of Background Independence. The classical part
of this requires just Lie's Mathematics, much of which is basic: i) Lie
derivatives to encode Relationalism. ii) Lie brackets for Closure giving Lie
algebraic structures. iii) Observables defined by a Lie brackets relation, in
the constrained canonical case as explicit PDEs to be solved using Lie's flow
method, and themselves forming Lie algebras. iv) Lattices of constraint
algebraic substructures induce dual lattices of observables subalgebras.
  The current Article focuses on two pieces of `higher Lie Theory' that are
also required. Preliminarily, we extend Dirac's Algorithm for Constraint
Closure to `Lie's Algorithm' for Generator Closure. 1) We then reinterpret
`passing families of theories through the Dirac Algorithm' - a method used for
Spacetime Construction (from space) and getting more structure from less
structure assumed more generally - as the Dirac Rigidity subcase of Lie
Rigidity. We also provide a Foundations of Geometry example of specifically Lie
rather than Dirac Rigidity, to illustrate merit in extending from Dirac to Lie
Algorithms. We point to such rigidity providing a partial cohomological (and
thus global) selection principle for the Comparative Theory of Background
Independence. 2) We finally pose the universal (theory-independent) analogue of
GR's Refoliation Invariance for the general Lie Theory: Reallocation of
Intermediary-Object Invariance. This is a commuting pentagon criterion: in
evolving from an initial object to a final object, does switching which
intermediary object one proceeds via amount to at most a difference by an
automorphism of the final object? We argue for this to also be a selection
principle in the Comparative Theory of Background Independence.

Recently it has been suggested that the majority of dark matter in the
universe resides in the form of Jupiter mass black holes distributed
cosmologically. This population makes itself apparent by microlensing high
redshift quasars and introducing pronounced variability into their observed
light curves. While several arguments dismissing this hypothesis have been
presented, a conclusive observational test is, alas, sadly lacking. In this
paper we investigate the effect of a cosmologically distributed population of
microlensing masses on galaxies at low to intermediate redshift. The
magnification of bright stars in these galaxies leads to small, but observable,
fluctuations in their surface brightness. The variability time scale for
Jupiter-mass lensing objects is of the order of a few months and this
population may be detected through a future space-based monitoring campaign of
a field containing $z \sim 0.5$ galaxies. The monitoring of galactic surface
brightness will provide an effective test of the nature of dark matter on
cosmological scales.

We study the non-equilibrium dynamics of a pair of qubits made of two-level
atoms separated in space with distance r and interacting with one common
electromagnetic field but not directly with each other. Our calculation makes a
weak coupling assumption, but no Born or Markov approximation. We derived a
non-Markovian master equation for the evolution of the reduced density matrix
of the two-qubit system after integrating out the electromagnetic field modes.
It contains a Markovian part with a Lindblad type operator and a nonMarkovian
contribution, the physics of which is the main focus of this study. We use the
concurrence function as a measure of quantum entanglement between the two
qubits. Two classes of states are studied in detail: Class A is a one parameter
family of states which are the superposition of the highest energy and Class B
states are linear combinations of the symmetric and the antisymmetric Bell
states. We obtain similar behavior for the Bell states as in earlier results
derived by using the Born-Markov approximation. However, in the Class A states
the behavior is qualitatively different: under the non-Markovian evolution we
do not see sudden death of quantum entanglement and subsequent revivals, except
when the qubits are sufficiently far apart. We provide explanations for such
differences of behavior both between these two classes of states and between
the predictions from the Markov and non-Markovian dynamics. We also study the
decoherence of this two-qubit system and find that the decoherence rate in the
case of one qubit initially in an excited state does not change significantly
with the qubits separation whereas it does for the case when one qubit is
initially in the ground state.

We consider the radiation-hydrodynamical formation of a dusty wall in the
circumnuclear regions of the Circinus Seyfert 2 galaxy. We focus on the
radiative flux force due to the circumnuclear starburst, the nuclear starburst
and the active galactic nucleus (AGN), and analyze the equilibrium
configuration and stability of the dusty gas in the circumnuclear regions. The
mass distributions used to model the gravitational fields are determined by the
observed rotation velocities of the stars and of the gas. Moreover, by using
the simple stellar evolution in the circumnuclear starburst, the bolometric
luminosity of the starburst is estimated as a function of time. As a result, it
is shown that the radiation force by the circumnuclear starburst does play an
important role to build up a radiatively-supported dusty wall of $\la$ 100 pc,
which obscures the circumnuclear regions with $A_V$ of a few magnitudes. It is
also found that the age of a circumnuclear starburst when the circumnuclear
regions are enshrouded by the dusty wall is constrained to be $\la 10^8$ yr,
which is consistent with the age observationally inferred for the starburst, $4
10^7$ yr $-1.5 10^8$ yr.

Initially identified as a neutron star candidate in the ROSAT All-Sky Survey, the object 1RXS J141256.0+792204 ("Calvera") was later found to be a 59-millisecond X-ray pulsar through two XMM-Newton observations (Zane et al. 2011). Intriguingly, the reported detection of this pulsar in Fermi gamma-ray data suggested no period derivative, which would place tight limits on its dipole magnetic field strength, spin-down luminosity, and distance—all pointing to unusually low values. This scenario would mean Calvera’s cooling age is significantly younger than its characteristic spin-down age, raising the possibility that it is either a mildly recycled pulsar or the first known "orphaned" central compact object (CCO). However, we demonstrate that the published Fermi timing solution does not successfully align the pulse phases between the two X-ray observations, strongly suggesting the gamma-ray detection is likely invalid. Further analysis of additional Fermi data also fails to confirm the gamma-ray signal. As a result, Calvera’s spin-down rate remains poorly constrained, leaving its classification among neutron star types unresolved. It may still be a normal pulsar, a mildly recycled pulsar, or an orphaned CCO.

We apply Reinforcement Learning (RL) to manage network congestion in datacenters. Effective congestion control can significantly reduce latency and increase total network throughput. So far, learning-based methods have not demonstrated real-world viability in this area. In practice, recent widely adopted solutions depend on fixed rule-based heuristics, which are validated only against a limited set of benchmarks. As a result, these heuristics often fail to adapt to unfamiliar conditions. In contrast, we design an RL-driven algorithm intended to generalize across various real-world datacenter setups. We address issues including partial observability, non-stationary dynamics, and multiple competing objectives. Additionally, we introduce a policy gradient method that uses the known form of the reward function to estimate its derivative, enhancing training stability. Our approach not only surpasses other common RL techniques but also adapts to situations not encountered during training. Tests run on a high-fidelity network simulator show that our method improves performance across several key metrics when compared to leading algorithms currently used in production datacenters. The algorithm is now being integrated into products and will replace heuristic methods in several of the world’s largest datacenter networks.

A recently developed general method for obtaining the diagonal representation of an arbitrary matrix-valued quantum Hamiltonian is applied to the specific case of Bloch electrons in an external electromagnetic field. The diagonal representation is derived as a series expansion up to second order in $\hbar$. This outcome provides the foundation for constructing the effective in-band Hamiltonian of interacting Bloch electrons that occupy distinct energy bands. Describing phenomena such as magnetic moment–moment interactions, which are mediated by the magnetic component of the full electromagnetic interaction, requires a calculation accurate to second order in $\hbar$. The analysis reveals that the electronic current consists of two parts: one originating from the velocity, and another that is a magnetic moment current analogous to the spin current for Dirac particles. This latter contribution drives the interaction between magnetic moments, much like the spin–spin interaction in the Breit Hamiltonian for interacting Dirac electrons.

Deep learning has made image classification a widely studied area, with many advanced models emerging annually. Yet there is growing concern over the emphasis on increasingly complex designs that drive performance metrics upward. Standard evaluation methods often miss critical weaknesses and biases, which robustness testing can reveal. To date, research on model robustness under distribution shifts has relied on carefully controlled datasets. However, these methods fail to assess how classifiers truly perform in real-world conditions—such as when presented with raw, web-crawled images from relevant categories. In this work, we conduct fine-grained classification on visually similar categories, identified using hierarchical knowledge. Testing across multiple convolutional and transformer-based architectures uncovers model robustness in this new scenario. We further apply hierarchical knowledge to analyze and interpret misclassifications, offering a detailed and flexible evaluation framework applicable to any classifier.

In recent years, galaxy surveys have sought potential features in the primordial power spectrum, as these could help clarify the nature of inflation. So far, no such features have been detected, indicating that any present must be relatively weak. Upcoming galaxy surveys, with their exceptional scale and precision, will be able to set tighter limits than ever before. Yet even if primordial features did exist in the early Universe, they would have been heavily suppressed by nonlinear structure formation at low redshifts, making them hard to observe directly. One promising approach to this problem is to reverse cosmic evolution—using reconstruction methods to approximate the original linear density field. Through a series of N-body simulations, we demonstrate that a recently developed nonlinear reconstruction technique can successfully recover damped oscillatory features from halo catalogs and enhance the precision of measured feature parameters, assuming such features are real. A Fisher analysis predicts that nonlinear reconstruction would substantially strengthen constraints on feature amplitude for a survey like DESI. Compared to other methods of improving constraints—such as expanding survey volume or probing additional scales—nonlinear reconstruction can deliver similar gains at reduced cost.

Recent observations of ridge-like structures in proton-proton (p-p) and proton-nucleus (p-A) angular correlations at RHIC and the LHC have been interpreted as evidence for collective motion in smaller collision systems. The argument suggests that if correlation structures accepted as flow signatures in nucleus-nucleus (A-A) collisions also appear in smaller systems, then collectivity—or flow—must extend to those smaller systems. However, this reasoning could be inverted to propose that such structures in A-A collisions may not necessarily indicate hydrodynamic flow.

I present spectrum, correlation, and fluctuation data from RHIC p-p and Au-Au collisions, along with p-p, p-Pb, and Pb-Pb results from the LHC, all well described by a two-component (soft + dijet) model of hadron production. Additionally, I provide evidence for a significant nonjet (NJ) quadrupole component (v₂) in p-p collisions, whose charged-particle multiplicity (nₕₕ) systematics are directly related to those of the A-A NJ quadrupole. Together, these findings indicate that soft, dijet, and NJ quadrupole components are distinct phenomena across all collision systems. This pattern is inconsistent with hadron production originating from a common bulk medium exhibiting collective motion (flow).

Analyzing peaks in weak lensing convergence maps offers a valuable method for probing dark matter halo properties and refining cosmological parameters. This work examines how the number of observable peaks and their variation with redshift are influenced by cluster dark matter halo profiles, and applies peak statistics to constrain the parameters of the mass–concentration (MC) relation. We explore the constraints the Euclid mission can place on the MC coefficients, including potential degeneracies with cosmological parameters. First, we compute the number of peaks and their redshift distribution for various MC relations. Results show that a steeper mass dependence and a larger normalization increase the number of detectable clusters, with the total peak count varying by up to 40% depending on the MC relation. Next, we conduct a Fisher matrix forecast to estimate uncertainties in both the MC relation parameters and cosmological parameters. Euclid’s peak number counts are found to constrain the normalization \(A_v\), mass slope \(B_v\), redshift slope \(C_v\), and intrinsic scatter \(\sigma_v\) of the MC relation with exceptional precision: \(\sigma(A_v)/A_v = 1\%\), \(\sigma(B_v)/B_v = 4\%\), \(\sigma(C_v)/C_v = 9\%\), and \(\sigma(\sigma_v)/\sigma_v = 1\%\) when all cosmological parameters are fixed. Relaxing this strict assumption weakens the constraints, but combining peak counts with Planck data or fixing only a subset of parameters recovers strong results. Such accuracy can shed light on competing models of structure formation and evolution, as well as the influence of baryons in cluster assembly. Alternatively, for a given MC relation, future peak counts combined with Planck could match the constraining power of current baryon acoustic oscillation and Type Ia supernova measurements.

We explore search strategies for an extended Higgs sector at the high-luminosity LHC14, focusing on final states with multiple top quarks. Within a Two Higgs Doublet Model, final states involving only top quarks—such as \(t\bar{t}\) and \(4t\)—are key channels for heavy Higgs bosons with masses above \(2m_t\) and at low \(\tan\beta\). Meanwhile, a \(2b2t\) final state becomes more significant at moderate \(\tan\beta\). In the \(t\bar{t}H\) channel with \(H \rightarrow t\bar{t}\), both single-lepton and three-lepton final states can yield statistically meaningful constraints at low \(\tan\beta\) for \(m_A\) up to roughly 750 GeV. However, when systematic uncertainties in the \(t\bar{t}\) background are included, the three-lepton channel proves more effective, though the exact sensitivity depends considerably on lepton misidentification rates. Additionally, neither \(2b2t\) nor \(t\bar{t}\) final states offer constraints on extra heavy Higgs bosons with couplings to top quarks weaker than the top Yukawa, due to expected systematic uncertainties in the \(t\bar{t}\) background.

This study evaluates the Public Safety Assessment (PSA), a risk assessment tool used in San Francisco and other areas to help judges determine whether defendants should be detained before trial. Using a mixed-methods approach—including stakeholder interviews and theoretical frameworks—we examine the values involved as pretrial justice becomes automated. After exploring the implications of delegating such decisions to technology, we outline both the advantages and constraints of the PSA and propose strategies to address its limitations. We then introduce the Handoff Tree, a new algorithmic model designed for pretrial decisions that intentionally accounts for some of the inherent shortcomings of risk assessment tools. This model provides each prediction with a corresponding error rate and transfers the decision back to the judge when uncertainty is too great. By making error rates transparent, the Handoff Tree seeks to reduce the effects of predictive disparities across race and gender, while also encouraging judges to scrutinize detention recommendations more carefully, particularly given the frequent occurrence of false positives.

This study investigates how the thickness of the cobalt layer influences magnetocrystalline anisotropy (MCA), the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI), and orbital moment anisotropy (OMA) in W/Co/Pt trilayers, aiming to understand their interrelationships. The MCA, which prefers magnetization perpendicular to the film plane, grows monotonically as the effective magnetic layer thickness ($t_\mathrm{eff}$) decreases. Meanwhile, the magnitude of the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya exchange constant ($|D|$) rises with reduced $t_\mathrm{eff}$ until approximately 1 nm, below which it declines. Both MCA and $|D|$ follow a $1/t_\mathrm{eff}$ scaling for $t_\mathrm{eff}$ above ~1 nm, pointing to an interfacial origin. While MCA continues to increase below ~1 nm, its rate of change slows.

To understand the thickness dependence of MCA and DMI, the OMA of cobalt in these trilayers was examined via x-ray magnetic circular dichroism (XMCD). A non-zero OMA emerges when $t_\mathrm{eff}$ falls below ~0.8 nm and intensifies with decreasing thickness more sharply than the corresponding MCA. This discrepancy indicates that contributions beyond OMA affect the MCA at small $t_\mathrm{eff}$. The OMA's thickness dependence further suggests that $|D|$ below ~1 nm is not linked to interfacial OMA. We attribute the reduction in interfacial DMI and, partially, MCA at low $t_\mathrm{eff}$ to strain or textural changes arising from cobalt growth on tungsten.

In recent years, researchers have designed and synthesized numerous molecular motors, which exhibit various forms of motion and are powered by diverse energy sources such as light, heat, or chemical reactions. Here we introduce a novel molecular motor design that uses an electric field to drive rotation and an electric current to detect the motion of a molecular dipole within a three-terminal single-molecule device. Central to this all-electronic approach is the molecule’s conjugated backbone, which both defines the rotational potential landscape and allows real-time monitoring of orientation through changes in conductivity. Quantum chemistry calculations demonstrate that this system offers precise control over the speed and continuity of rotation, integrating electrical and mechanical control at the molecular scale across a broad temperature range. Additionally, chemical modifications can adjust all essential device parameters, opening the door to a wide array of new experiments on molecular motors.

The swelling and collapse of weakly charged polymers in the presence of certain salts show complex experimental behavior that is difficult to classify. Using a simplified simulation model with implicit solvent and explicit salt, we focus on ion-specific interactions between ions and a single weakly charged polyelectrolyte to provide a qualitative explanation for these observations. We show that ion-specific screening and bridging effects lead to collapse at low salt concentrations, while the same strong direct ion-specific interactions induce re-entrant swelling at high concentrations. In line with experiments, we identify a specific salt concentration at which the salting-out effectiveness of anions switches from a reverse to a direct Hofmeister ordering. At this "isospheric point," ion-specific effects disappear. Additionally, by introducing further simplifications, we develop an ion-specific mean-field model for the collapse transition that matches the simulation results quantitatively. This study highlights how sensitive charged polymers are to the addition of particular salts and offers useful insights for guiding future experiments.

The K2 extended mission, or Kepler's second phase, has delivered a rich collection of planetary candidates orbiting stars that are generally brighter than those observed in the original mission. These candidates are well-suited for detailed spectroscopic follow-up and accurate mass measurements, which help build empirical mass-radius relationships. Of special interest are planets with sizes between Earth and Neptune—the most common type found around Sun-like stars with orbital periods under a few years. Among numerous K2 candidates, we identified a multi-planet system around EPIC246471491, hosting four planets ranging from roughly twice Earth’s size to nearly Neptune’s size.

Using precise radial velocity data from the CARMENES and HARPS-N spectrographs, we measured the masses of the planets in this system. Our observations allow us to determine the masses of the two inner planets with better than 15% precision and set upper mass limits for the two outer planets. EPIC246471491b has a mass of 9.68 Earth masses and a radius of 2.59 Earth radii, giving a mean density of 3.07 g/cm³. EPIC246471491c has a mass of 15.68 Earth masses, a radius of 3.53 Earth radii, and a mean density of 19.5 g/cm³. For EPIC246471491d (radius 2.48 Earth radii) and EPIC246471491e (radius 1.95 Earth radii), the upper mass limits are 6.5 and 10.7 Earth masses, respectively.

This system includes a near twin of Neptune in mass and radius, two sub-Neptunes with markedly different densities and likely compositions, and a fourth outer planet positioned within the super-Earth/sub-Neptune radius gap. Further comparative studies of this planetary system could offer valuable insights into planet formation and provide a useful test for theories of atmospheric escape

Two-dimensional materials are valued for their exceptional characteristics, which enable diverse applications. Their electrochemical stability, minimal electrical resistance, and large specific surface area are especially advantageous for use in supercapacitors. This work presents a straightforward method for creating flexible, biodegradable, and inexpensive supercapacitors by hand-drawing or rubbing traces of pencil, molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂), titanium trisulfide (TiS₃), or franckeite onto paper. Among the tested electrodes, pencil-drawn paper exhibits the highest capacitance, reaching approximately 6.39 F/g. Notably, electrodes combining pencil with MoS₂, TiS₃, or franckeite show significant enhancements, including extended cycle life, due to synergistic effects and a greater number of accessible active sites within the heterostructures. Additionally, the assembled symmetric solid-state supercapacitors maintain their functionality when bent, highlighting their strong suitability for wearable and flexible devices.

Users' responses to content on social media are shaped by context, with emotions and mood heavily influencing these reactions. This has led to platforms becoming saturated with opinionated material. While various methods and applications are continually being created to better leverage this data, challenges persist due to the data's inherent nature, the diversity of platforms, and the ever-changing behavior of users online. Accurately determining a user's emotional state before they post a comment remains difficult. This study presents a semi-supervised, multilingual approach to emotion detection, utilizing the intersection between Facebook reactions and textual data. Using this emotion detection system, we assess the potential of combining emotional and user behavior features for identifying sarcasm. Over one million English and Chinese comments from more than 62,000 public Facebook page posts were gathered and analyzed, with experimental results demonstrating satisfactory performance.

Background: When initial proof-of-principle trials show promise but the best implementation approach is still unclear, trials that both measure intervention effects and deliver public health benefits are valuable. Cluster randomized trials (CRTs) capture direct and indirect intervention effects, with indirect effects largely shaped by contact networks within and between clusters. We introduce a new category of connectivity-informed trial designs that use network data to enhance public health impact while maintaining the ability to measure intervention effects.

Methods: We examine CRTs in which enrollment order is determined by the number of connections between individuals across clusters—either all inter-cluster ties or only ties to untreated clusters. These designs include variations similar to traditional Parallel and Stepped Wedge approaches. We also allow control clusters to be temporarily excluded from re-randomization. To evaluate epidemic control and the power to detect vaccine effects, we simulate vaccination trials during an SEIR-type epidemic using a network-based agent-based model.

Results: In simulations, connectivity-informed designs achieve lower peak infectiousness and reduce cumulative incidence by 20% compared to traditional designs, though they have minimal effect on epidemic duration and reduce the power to detect incidence differences between clusters. However, even a short holdback period largely restores the statistical power lost relative to conventional methods.

Conclusion: Using cluster connectivity information in CRT design can boost public health impact, particularly during acute outbreaks, with only a modest reduction in power to identify effective interventions.

We investigate a two-layer square lattice gas at half-filling, driven into non-equilibrium steady states by a strong but finite “electric” field. By introducing unequal couplings within the layers, we extended the phase diagram reported by Hill, Zia, and Schmittmann (1996). Our results show that the tricritical point—separating the stripped (S) phase (stable for positive interlayer interactions J₃), the filled-empty (FE) phase (stable for negative J₃), and the disordered (D) phase—shifts deeper into the negative J₃ region as the coupling transverse to the driving field grows. Along the S-FE boundary, many transient states leading to the S phase were observed to persist for extended periods. We further examined whether the universality class of the D-FE transition under driving remains Ising. Simulations yield a critical exponent γ ≈ 1.75, while the ratio γ/ν is near 2.0. This suggests that the second-order D-FE transition may deviate from Ising behavior close to criticality, where first-order-like transitions between the FE phase and a related “local minimum” state appear repeatedly during individual simulation runs.

We investigate the role of stellar collisions in the formation and growth of high-mass stars using direct N-body simulations of star clusters embedded in gas. The simulations begin with clusters in virial equilibrium, composed of both gas and protostars. Protostars accrete mass at various rates, with the gas reservoir decreasing as stellar mass increases. Throughout the simulations, we track stellar collisions and analyze their impact on the development of massive stars and the emergence of runaway stars.

Our results show that a substantial number of collisions occur only in clusters with initial half-mass radii smaller than 0.1 parsecs. After expelling their natal gas, such clusters become more compact than observed young, massive open clusters. Moreover, collisions tend to produce a single runaway star rather than generating many high-mass stars across a broad mass spectrum. Consequently, we find that massive stars form primarily through gas accretion, with stellar collisions playing at most a minor role. However, collisions during the pre-main sequence phase could contribute to the formation of the most massive stars in extremely dense clusters and might also aid in forming intermediate-mass black holes with masses up to several hundred solar masses.

The remarkable efficiency gains of quantum computers compared to classical ones prompt a fundamental question: to what extent does this advantage stem from quantum mechanics, and how much arises from probabilistic elements? To explore this distinction, we introduce the concept of a chemical computer based on classical, probabilistic reaction-diffusion (RD) processes. Governed by nonequilibrium statistical mechanics, such a system can represent superpositions of empty and occupied states as probabilistic mixtures. Using these probabilistic states as a computational basis, RD computers—whose operations satisfy a required semigroup property—can emulate certain quantum logic gates and perform teleportation-like procedures with entangled states, phenomena often considered exclusive to quantum systems. Furthermore, with a nonlinear extension, these RD computers could even clone arbitrary states, an operation famously prohibited in conventional quantum computation.

Accurate plasma models, which rely on comprehensive atomic data, are crucial for interpreting observed cosmic spectra. In the X-ray band, diagnostic lines such as the H-like Lyman series and He-like triplets provide powerful tools for measuring the physical properties of diverse astrophysical plasmas. Electron-impact excitation is a fundamental atomic process responsible for forming these key H-like and He-like diagnostic lines. However, the electron-impact excitation data used in widely adopted plasma codes—AtomDB, CHIANTI, and SPEX—often show discrepancies. This study presents a systematic calculation of electron-impact excitation data for H-like and He-like ions with atomic numbers from Z=6 to 30 (carbon through zinc). For each ion, radiation-damped R-matrix intermediate coupling frame transformation calculations were performed, including configurations up to $n=6$. The new data are compared with the three plasma codes and existing literature to evaluate their quality, which is important for current and upcoming high-resolution X-ray spectrometers.

Reinforcement learning (RL) has attracted growing attention, particularly after deep Q-learning (DQN) showed it could match human-level performance on video game benchmarks. Today, training neural networks in these complex settings generally depends on gradient-based optimization. While Bayesian deep learning offers other possibilities, most methods still use gradient-based approaches and often struggle to scale to environments like Atari games. Additionally, none of these techniques enable analytical inference for the neural network’s weights and biases. This paper introduces an adaptation of the temporal difference Q-learning framework to work with tractable approximate Gaussian inference (TAGI), a method that learns network parameters through a closed-form analytical solution. Experiments with both on- and off-policy reinforcement learning show that TAGI achieves performance similar to networks trained with backpropagation, using fewer hyperparameters and no gradient-based optimization.

Aspect sentiment triplet extraction (ASTE) is a growing area within fine-grained opinion mining, focused on detecting aspects in review sentences together with their related opinion expressions and sentiments. This task involves several components—such as extracting opinion entities, detecting relations, and classifying sentiment—making it essential yet difficult to effectively model the connections between them. To meet this challenge, we reformulate ASTE as a multi-turn machine reading comprehension (MTMRC) problem and introduce a bidirectional MRC (BMRC) framework. Our approach employs three query types: non-restrictive extraction queries, restrictive extraction queries, and sentiment classification queries, which help link the various subtasks. Additionally, because an aspect sentiment triplet can originate from either an aspect or an opinion expression, we implement a bidirectional MRC structure. In one direction, the model sequentially identifies aspects, opinion expressions, and sentiments to form triplets; in the other, it begins with opinion expressions, then aspects, and finally sentiments. This bidirectional design allows the two processes to reinforce each other, leading to more thorough triplet extraction. We evaluate our method through extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets, where results show that BMRC delivers state-of-the-art performance.

Within the intracluster medium (ICM) of galaxies, heat transfer perpendicular to magnetic field lines is highly restricted. This anisotropic conduction, combined with a large-scale temperature gradient, drives turbulence in the outskirts of galaxy clusters through the magneto-thermal instability (MTI), providing an explanation for observed ICM turbulence. Here we extend our study of the MTI using three-dimensional simulations with the Boussinesq code SNOOPY, addressing two limitations of our earlier two-dimensional work: an inverse energy cascade and the absence of dynamo action. Unlike in 2D, we find minimal energy transfer across scales—most energy dissipates near the injection scale—and turbulent eddies are elongated vertically at or below the thermal conduction length, becoming more isotropic on larger scales. As in 2D, however, the saturated turbulent energy levels and integral scale follow distinct power laws that depend on thermal diffusivity, temperature gradient, and buoyancy frequency. We also demonstrate that the MTI amplifies magnetic fields via a fluctuation dynamo to equipartition strengths, provided the integral scale of MTI turbulence exceeds the viscous dissipation scale. Finally, our scaling laws align with existing ICM turbulence observations if the thermal conductivity is reduced by roughly a factor of 10 from the Spitzer value, and we show that stable stratification on global cluster scales substantially limits the vertical elongation of MTI-driven motions.

This study presents the synthesis and characterization of polycrystalline ZrB12 and MgB2, focusing on the temperature-dependent resistivity R(T), penetration depth λ(T), and upper critical magnetic field Hc2(T). In the normal state, both materials exhibit simple metallic behavior with Bloch–Grüneisen resistivity and a relatively low resistive Debye temperature for ZrB12 (TR = 280 K) compared to MgB2 (TR = 900 K). Superconductivity in ZrB12 emerges at Tc = 6.0 K, as seen in both R(T) and λ(T). At low temperatures, λ(T) follows an exponential trend in MgB2 thin films and ceramic pellets, while ZrB12 shows an approximately linear dependence below Tc/2. These results suggest s-wave pairing in MgB2 and possible d-wave pairing in ZrB12. Fitting the data yields a reduced energy gap 2Δ(0)/kTc = 1.6 for MgB2 films and pellets, consistent with reported values for the three-dimensional π-sheets of its Fermi surface. In contrast to conventional theories, Hc2(T) for ZrB12 displays a linear temperature dependence, with Hc2(0) = 0.15 T.

Coulomb drag in nanoscale conductors holds both fundamental and practical significance. In this theoretical study, we examine drag in a double quantum-dot (QD) setup, where a biased drive QD and an unbiased drag QD are connected through a direct interdot Coulomb interaction. We find that charge fluctuations in the drive QD drive the Coulomb drag, and we illustrate how the characteristics of the related quantum noise differentiate it from other mechanisms, such as shot-noise driven drag in weakly interacting quantum conductor circuits. In the strong-interaction regime, which displays an orbital ("pseudospin") Kondo effect, drag is controlled by charge fluctuations resulting from pseudospin-flip cotunneling events. Kondo correlations suppress these pseudospin-flip processes, reducing drag at low bias and creating a zero-bias anomaly in the second-order differential transconductance. Additionally, we demonstrate that drag reaches its peak when the interdot interaction strength aligns with the lead couplings. These results contribute to the understanding of drag in QD systems and offer testable predictions across various transport conditions.

Accurate and efficient point cloud registration remains difficult, as noise and high point counts complicate the search for correspondences. Most current approaches still depend on correspondence matching, leaving this an open research problem. To address it, we introduce a new data-driven registration method that applies deep generative neural networks to point cloud alignment. The core idea is to directly generate aligned point clouds from two input sets, a capability valuable for applications such as 3D matching and search. We realize this through an end-to-end generative neural network that produces aligned point clouds, incorporating three key innovations. First, a point multi-perception layer (MLP) mixer (PointMixer) network effectively preserves both global and local structural information across multiple levels within each point cloud. Second, a feature interaction module integrates information between the two point clouds. Third, a parallel and differentiable sample consensus method computes the transformation matrix for the input point clouds based on the generated alignment results. This generative network is trained within a GAN framework, preserving data distribution and structural similarity. Evaluations on the ModelNet40 and 7Scene datasets show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency. Specifically, it reduces registration error (CD) by a factor of two and cuts runtime by a factor of twelve compared to leading correspondence-based methods.

Node-link diagrams are a common method for visualizing networks. Yet, as a graph grows in complexity, even the most effective layout algorithms tend to produce cluttered, hairball-like visuals. To keep these diagrams usable, simplification techniques like aggregation or interactive filtering are often needed. So far, limited data has existed on when node-link diagrams become ineffective or how visual complexity influences cognitive load. To address this, we carried out a controlled experiment to identify workload limits for a task demanding detailed topological understanding: finding the shortest path between two nodes. We evaluated performance across graphs ranging from 25 to 175 nodes with different densities, gathering data on accuracy, response time, subjective feedback, and physiological signals (EEG, pupil dilation, and heart rate variability). To our knowledge, this is the first study in network visualization to incorporate physiological measures. Our findings reveal that participants struggle significantly with locating the shortest path in high-density diagrams containing over 50 nodes, and even in low-density graphs with more than 100 nodes. EEG data indicated distinct patterns of brain activity between difficult and easy tasks. Cognitive load rose with increasing difficulty up to a point, after which it declined—likely because participants disengaged. We also examined how global layout features (like size or edge crossings) and shortest-path characteristics (such as length or straightness) affect task difficulty. Overall, global features had a stronger influence than those specific to the shortest path.

Effective bug triaging is crucial for the success of collaborative software projects. This task can be especially demanding in open source software (OSS) projects, which often rely on many part-time contributors with limited experience. We introduce a practical approach for identifying valid bug reports—those that describe real bugs, are not duplicates, and provide sufficient detail for immediate handling. Our method classifies reports using nine metrics that assess a reporter’s social connections within the project’s collaboration network.

We test this approach using over 700,000 bug reports from the Bugzilla systems of four major OSS communities, spanning more than a decade. In projects with the lowest proportion of valid reports, we show that a reporter’s position in the collaboration network strongly predicts report quality. Building on this insight, we design an automated classification system suitable for integration into bug tracking platforms and evaluate its performance across the studied communities. A support vector machine (SVM) trained on the nine social metrics achieves up to 90.3% precision and 38.9% recall in identifying valid bug reports. These results mark a significant advance over earlier studies on automated early bug detection.

Our work underscores the value of applying quantitative social measures in collaborative software engineering and points toward a wider integration of social awareness into support infrastructure design.

We begin by reviewing the scaling argument used in a renormalization-group (RG) analysis for systems undergoing dimensional reduction in strong magnetic fields. It is shown that a four-Fermi operator describing low-energy excitations remains marginal, regardless of the coupling strength in the underlying theory. Next, we derive a scale-dependent effective four-Fermi interaction arising from screened photon exchange in the weak-coupling regime, and develop an RG framework that properly incorporates screening effects. In this approach, the RG flow from ultraviolet to infrared scales is divided into two distinct regimes separated by the screening mass scale.

By demonstrating precise agreement between the dynamical mass gaps obtained from the RG and Schwinger-Dyson equations, we argue for an equivalence between these two methods. Using QED and the Nambu–Jona-Lasinio model as examples, we illustrate how the characteristics of the interactions influence the mass gap, and emphasize the need to account for the intrinsic energy-scale dependence in the underlying theories when determining the mass gap. These findings are anticipated to aid in understanding magnetic catalysis in QCD.

The analysis and filtering of Ito stochastic differential systems have been studied extensively. Traditionally, such filtering for Ito SDEs is addressed through the Kushner equation. Departing from this classical method, the present work introduces a filtering approach for stochastic differential systems influenced by weakly colored noise. This process can be viewed as a special case of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process. The theoretical foundation builds on Stratonovich’s pioneering application of perturbation theory to noisy dynamical systems, integrated with the concept of filtering density evolution. Using the filtering density evolution equation, the stochastic evolution of conditional moments is derived. To evaluate the proposed filtering theory, a scalar Duffing system driven by an OU process is examined. The effectiveness of the filtering algorithm is assessed through numerical simulations with four distinct sets of initial conditions and system parameters.

Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have emerged as an effective approach for high-quality video compression. Current methods, however, often fail to fully leverage temporal redundancy, resulting in slow encoding. They also rely on fixed architectures that cannot easily adapt to longer videos or higher resolutions. To overcome these limitations, we introduce NIRVANA, a method that processes videos in frame groups, with a dedicated network per group performing patch-based prediction. This approach shares computation across spatial and temporal dimensions within each group, significantly reducing encoding time. The representation is learned autoregressively: networks for the current group are initialized using weights from the previous group’s model. For greater efficiency, we quantize network parameters during training, eliminating the need for post-processing steps like pruning or quantization. Evaluated on the UVG dataset, NIRVANA raises encoding quality from 37.36 to 37.70 PSNR and accelerates encoding by 12×, all while maintaining the same compression rate. Unlike earlier video INR techniques, which face challenges with higher resolutions and longer sequences, our method scales naturally thanks to its patch-based and autoregressive design. It also supports variable bitrate compression by adjusting to different levels of inter-frame motion. Additionally, NIRVANA achieves 6× faster decoding and scales efficiently with multiple GPUs, making it suitable for diverse real-world applications.

Elastic metasurfaces provide a powerful method for manipulating stress waves at scales smaller than their wavelength. However, their practical application is often limited by a naturally narrow operating bandwidth. This work proposes intentional nonlocality as a foundational principle for designing passive elastic metasurfaces with exceptionally broad operational bandwidth. Nonlocal behavior is realized by introducing long-range interactions—similar to those found in nonlocal material microstructures—between groups of resonant unit cells within the metasurface. These interactions are produced through precisely engineered flexible elements, whose geometry and local dynamics generate highly complex transfer functions linking multiple units. The resulting nonlocal coupling allows phase gradient profiles to vary with the incident wave’s wavenumber.

Key design parameters are identified and their effects evaluated using a combination of semi-analytical and numerical models. The nonlocal metasurface concept is validated numerically and experimentally by implementing a total-internal-reflection design in a thin plate waveguide. The results demonstrate the viability of this intentionally nonlocal approach and its capacity for fully passive, broadband wave control.

In a recent paper [Irgaziev, Phys. Rev. C 91, 024002 (2015)], we introduced a new explicit expression for the asymptotic normalization coefficient (ANC) derived from the S-matrix pole method (SMP), which applies only to resonances. This approach was tested on low-energy resonant states in nucleon+α and α+¹²C systems, with SMP results compared to those from the effective-range expansion (EFE) method.

In the current work, we apply both the SMP and EFE methods, supplemented by Padé approximation, to examine the excited 2⁺ resonant states of ⁸Be. Our analysis reveals an inconsistency between the description of experimental αα scattering phase shifts and the reported resonant energy of the 2⁺ state in ⁸Be. Using the EFE method, we also compute the ANC for the ⁸Be ground 0⁺ state, which has a very small width. This ANC value aligns closely with results from the established analytical expression for narrow resonances.

Additionally, for the 1⁻ and 3⁻ states of α+¹²C, SMP outcomes are compared with Padé-approximation results. We observe that the Padé approximation yields a better description of resonance widths than the EFE method alone. The EFE approach is further employed to calculate ANCs for the bound 0⁺ ground state of ¹⁶O, as well as for the excited 1⁻ and 2⁺ levels near the α+¹²C threshold.

Density functional theory was applied to investigate the crystal, electronic, and magnetic structures of ordered double perovskites A₂NRuO₆ (A₂ = Ba₂, BaLa; N = V, Cr, Fe). In the doped variants, the A-site was substituted with a 1:1 mixture of Ba and La cations, forming BaLaNRuO₆. All A₂NRuO₆ compounds adopt a cubic structure with space group Fm-3m and tilt system a⁰a⁰a⁰.

Electronic and magnetic properties were calculated using the full-potential linear muffin-tin orbital method, employing both the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and GGA+U, which includes on-site electron correlation. Within GGA, Ba₂NRuO₆ exhibits half-metallic, semiconducting, and metallic behavior for N = V, Cr, and Fe, respectively. These all become half-metallic when A₂ = BaLa. In contrast, the GGA+U approach yields clear half-metallicity in all compounds except Ba₂FeRuO₆, which shows insulating behavior.

Magnetic structure calculations indicate that A₂NRuO₆ compounds are ferrimagnetic for N = V and Cr, but become ferromagnetic for N = Fe. The V³⁺, Cr³⁺, Fe³⁺, and Ru⁵⁺ ions adopt high-spin magnetic moment states due to antiferromagnetic coupling.

Intelligent transportation systems (ITS) depend on real-time traffic data—such as vehicle categories like cars, trucks, and buses—for functions like traffic monitoring on major roads and highways. Conventional methods using in-vehicle GPS sensors or camera networks often compromise driver privacy or involve high setup costs. This paper presents SenseMag, a low-cost approach that identifies vehicle types using two non-invasive magnetic sensors placed on a straight road segment. SenseMag removes noise and segments the magnetic signals based on the precise times a vehicle reaches and leaves each sensor node. It then applies a hierarchical recognition model: first estimating speed, then determining vehicle length using that speed along with sampling cycles and the distance between sensors. Once vehicle length is established and temporal/spectral features are extracted from the signals, SenseMag classifies the vehicle type. The design of filters, features, and hyper-parameters incorporates semi-automated learning techniques. Extensive real-world testing on highways in Shenzhen, China, demonstrates that SenseMag exceeds existing methods in both classification accuracy and the detail of vehicle types distinguished—identifying 7 categories compared to 4 in prior work. Specifically, field experiments confirm that SenseMag achieves at least 90% accuracy in vehicle type classification and keeps vehicle length classification error below 5%.

This paper introduces an adaptive deep density approximation method, ADDA-KR, for solving steady-state Fokker-Planck equations. These equations are often high-dimensional and defined over unbounded domains, posing challenges for conventional grid-based numerical techniques. Our approach employs KRnet, a novel flow-based generative model built on the Knothe-Rosenblatt rearrangement. KRnet generates a family of probability density functions suitable as solution candidates for Fokker-Planck problems, offering reduced sensitivity to dimensionality compared to traditional methods and enabling efficient estimation of high-dimensional densities. An adaptive sampling scheme is developed to produce effective stochastic collocation points for approximating the equation, iteratively generating samples using the current density estimate at each step. The general ADDA-KR framework is presented, and its accuracy and efficiency are confirmed through numerical experiments.

Early detection of faults is essential in the operation and maintenance of photovoltaic plants to sustain productivity and extend the service life of components. Among various defects, anomalies at the cell level can cause significant failures and may gradually impact neighboring PV modules. Such subtle defects are typically identified using high-resolution electroluminescence imaging. However, gathering these images is challenging, which has restricted the amount of available data. To address this limitation, this study employs multiple data sources and augmentation methods. Existing advanced detection approaches extract only basic information from individual PV cell images, and their effectiveness depends heavily on the training data at hand. This article introduces an end-to-end deep learning system that detects, locates, and segments cell-level anomalies in complete photovoltaic modules using EL images. The modular pipeline integrates three deep learning methods: 1. object detection (with a modified Faster-RNN), 2. image classification (using EfficientNet), and 3. weakly supervised segmentation (via an autoencoder). Its modular design enables the integration of future advancements in deep learning models and supports the addition of new capabilities.

This paper addresses the challenge of designing a meta-learning algorithm for online control that can transfer knowledge across a sequence of \( N \) related control tasks.
