To address this, we propose a comprehensive framework for FDI risk assessment. This framework integrates the costs of system intrusion—evaluated through a weighted graph analysis—with a physical vulnerability assessment based on line overloads. Simulations are conducted on an IEEE 14-bus system with an overlaid RTU network to realistically model intrusion risks. The cyber component examines multiple intrusion pathways, such as meter compromise, RTU compromise, and hybrid attacks. Following intrusion, the physical reinforcement model evaluates the degree of topology adjustment needed to prevent branch overloads from an optimized attack vector.

Stratified random sampling (SRS) is a widely adopted method for approximate query processing, offering precise aggregate estimates from a small sample. A central challenge in SRS is determining how to distribute a fixed sample size across various strata. Neyman allocation addresses this by minimizing estimate variance, but it relies on the assumption that every stratum is abundant—that is, each contains ample data points to sample from. In practice, this assumption often fails: even with a large overall dataset, certain strata may be limited in size.

We introduce VOILA, an offline approach that allocates sample sizes to strata in a way that optimizes variance, even when some strata are bounded. We then examine SRS in the context of streaming data, where records arrive continuously. We establish a lower bound, proving that any streaming SRS algorithm must, in the worst case, have variance at least an Ω(r) factor from optimal, with r representing the number of strata. To address this, we propose S-VOILA, a practical streaming algorithm that locally optimizes variance in its sample allocation across strata.

Experiments on real and synthetic datasets demonstrate that VOILA can reduce variance by a factor of 1.4 to 50.0 compared to Neyman allocation. Moreover, the streaming algorithm S-VOILA achieves variance typically close to that of VOILA, which benefits from having the entire dataset available in advance.

We introduce a framework for establishing upper limits on the number of regions over which feed-forward ReLU neural networks are piecewise affine. This approach uses an inductive method that tracks how many such regions correspond to each possible image dimension within the network layers. Starting from a single region matching the input dimension, the method propagates information about region counts per dimension layer by layer. This propagation relies on a recursive relation that determines, for a given input region of a certain dimensionality, how many output regions of each dimension can be created by a subsequent layer of specified width.

The resulting bound depends on the number of layers, their widths, and additional parameters that drive the recursion. It is expressed as the $L1$-norm of the last column in a product of matrices. This formulation unifies several earlier bounds: by selecting appropriate recursion parameters—and thus defining the matrices—the bounds from Montúfar (2014, 2017) and Serra et al. (2017) emerge as special cases. For the strongest of these, the bound from Serra et al., the matrix representation offers new clarity. Using explicit formulas for a Jordan-like decomposition of the relevant matrices, we derive tighter asymptotic results when the number of layers with a fixed width grows arbitrarily large.

Four-dimensional supersymmetric Yang-Mills (SYM) theories display a variety of interesting non-perturbative effects that can be explored through Monte Carlo lattice simulations. Yet lattice regularization explicitly breaks supersymmetry, typically demanding a fine-tuning of many parameters to properly extrapolate the theory to the continuum limit. For this reason, it is valuable to maintain on the lattice as many symmetries of the original continuum action as possible. For example, chiral symmetry prevents additive renormalization of the fermion mass. A (modified) form of chiral symmetry can be preserved exactly when the Dirac operator satisfies the Ginsparg-Wilson relation. In this work, we carry out an exploratory non-perturbative investigation of N=1 supersymmetric Yang-Mills theory using the overlap formalism, which maintains chiral symmetry at nonzero lattice spacings. N=1 SYM serves as an ideal testing ground for extending our studies to more complicated supersymmetric theories, since the only parameter requiring tuning is the gluino mass. Overlap fermions thus enable simulations without fine-tuning. We contrast our method with earlier studies of the same theory and provide clear evidence for gluino condensation.

We present a new class of sandwich panels that are exceptionally lightweight and stiff, designed specifically for photophoretic levitation. Their mechanical performance is evaluated through computational modeling and micro-mechanical experiments. Each panel is constructed from two face sheets joined by a core made of hollow cylindrical ligaments arranged in a hexagonal, honeycomb-like pattern. Computational analysis reveals that these panels offer significantly greater bending stiffness and buckling resistance than comparable designs with basketweave cores, and their behavior aligns well with Uflyand-Mindlin plate theory. By adjusting the ratio of face sheet thickness to ligament wall thickness, panels can be produced with a bending stiffness exceeding that of a solid plate of equal area density by more than five orders of magnitude. Through a scalable microfabrication method, we show that panels up to 3x3 cm² in size and with a density of 20 kg/m³ can be fabricated within hours. Micro-mechanical tests, conducted by deflecting microfabricated cantilevered panels with a nanoindenter, yield bending stiffness measurements that closely match computational predictions. This agreement confirms precise control over the dimensions, geometry, and properties of the manufactured panels.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) offers a detailed, noninvasive assessment of the heart and vascular system, though its clinical use is often limited by lengthy scan times. This study introduces and evaluates a plug-and-play (PnP) approach for reconstructing CMR images from undersampled multi-coil data. To leverage the rich structural information in cardiac images, the PnP framework incorporates a deep learning–based denoiser trained on spatiotemporal patches from high-quality, breath-held cine scans. This "PnP-DL" method alternates between enforcing data consistency and applying denoising steps.

We assessed the reconstruction performance of PnP-DL against compressed sensing (CS) using eight breath-held and ten real-time free-breathing cardiac cine datasets. For breath-held data, PnP-DL outperformed standard CS methods by more than one decibel. In real-time free-breathing cases, where a definitive reference is unavailable, PnP-DL achieved better ratings in visual evaluations. These findings suggest PnP-DL could help accelerate real-time CMR acquisitions.

Terahertz (THz)-band communication is widely regarded as a foundational technology for future wireless networks, capable of supporting diverse applications with high data demands and strict latency requirements. Recent progress in optical, electronic, and plasmonic transceiver design has made the development of integrated, adaptive, and efficient THz systems increasingly feasible. This paper offers a forward-looking perspective on the shift from the historical "THz gap" to an emerging "THz rush" in the coming years. We argue that the impact of the THz band will stem not only from its potential for high data rates, but more significantly from the interplay among THz sensing, imaging, and localization. We begin by examining the distinct characteristics of each of these applications in the THz range. Next, we demonstrate how their integration can improve environment-aware performance in scenarios beyond 5G. Finally, we explore practical implementation considerations for merging these applications, with a focus on shared versus dedicated resource allocation and the contribution of machine learning.

We present the detection of millihertz quasi-periodic oscillations (mHz QPOs) in the high-inclination atoll neutron-star low-mass X-ray binary EXO 0748–676, a source known for its X-ray bursts, using data from the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer (RXTE). This type of oscillation, first identified in three other neutron-star LMXBs, is thought to arise from a particular mode of nuclear burning on the neutron star surface. From the full set of RXTE archival observations of this source, we identified significant mHz QPOs (above 3σ) in 11 observations, with frequencies ranging from approximately 5 to 13 mHz. On two occasions, the QPO frequency decreased over time; in one instance, the oscillations ceased at the start of an X-ray burst, consistent with behavior seen in other systems. Analysis of the X-ray colors indicates that EXO 0748–676 was in a soft spectral state during the QPO detections. With these findings, EXO 0748–676 becomes the sixth source known to exhibit mHz QPOs linked to marginally stable burning and the second to display a systematic frequency drift. The results imply that the mechanism driving the drift may be active whenever mHz QPOs are observed in what is termed the intermediate state.

Acquiring the technical proficiency needed to perform surgery is a highly demanding process. Video-based assessment provides surgeons with feedback on their technical skills to support learning and improvement. At present, this feedback relies mainly on manual video review—a time-consuming process that makes it difficult to track a surgeon’s progress across numerous cases. This paper presents a motion-based method for automatically evaluating surgical skill directly from operative video. Our approach begins by reliably tracking surgical instruments to generate motion trajectories, which are then used to predict a surgeon’s technical skill level. The tracking algorithm incorporates a simple yet effective re-identification module that reduces ID-switch errors compared to other leading methods. This is essential for producing dependable tool trajectories, especially when instruments frequently leave and re-enter the frame or are temporarily obscured. For classification, we use a state-of-the-art self-attention transformer network that captures both short- and long-term motion patterns vital for skill evaluation. We test our method on the in-vivo Cholec80 dataset, using expert-rated GOALS skill assessments of the Calot Triangle Dissection as a quantitative measure of skill. Our comparison shows that transformer-based skill assessment, combined with the proposed tracking method, outperforms traditional machine learning approaches. The results indicate that motion trajectories derived from reliable tracking are valuable for evaluating surgeon skill using only video data.

We report the initial mid-infrared spectrum of the ultraluminous infrared galaxy FSC 10214+4724 at redshift z=2.2856, acquired using the Infrared Spectrograph on the Spitzer Space Telescope. Covering rest wavelengths from 2.3 to 11.5 microns, the spectrum reveals several important emission and absorption lines. Its most striking characteristic is silicate emission at a rest wavelength of 10 microns. We also observe an unresolved emission line at 7.65 microns, attributed to [NeVI], and a partially resolved feature at 5.6 microns, interpreted as a blend of [Mg VII] and [Mg V]. Notably, the spectrum lacks strong PAH emission features. An upper limit of 0.1 microns is placed on the equivalent width of the 6.2 micron PAH line, with no corresponding 7.7 micron feature detected.

Fitting the spectral energy distribution with semi-empirical models indicates that approximately 45% of the bolometric luminosity comes from cold dust at 50 K, about half from warm dust at 190 K, and the remaining 5% from hot dust at 640 K. This hot dust component is necessary to account for the blue slope of the steep mid-infrared continuum. The spectrum’s red continuum, strong silicate emission, minimal PAH emission, and absence of silicate absorption distinguish FSC 10214+4724 from most other ULIRGs or AGN observed with IRS to date. These seemingly inconsistent traits could be explained by a highly magnified AGN due to gravitational lensing, which obscures an underlying starburst that dominates the emission but exhibits unusually faint PAH features.

In parameterized graph edge modification problems, we start with a graph \(G\), an integer \(k\), and a typically well-structured graph class \(\mathcal{G}\). The question is whether \(G\) can be transformed into a graph \(G' \in \mathcal{G}\) by adding or removing at most \(k\) edges. Over recent decades, such problems have attracted significant interest.

This paper investigates the existence of small kernels for edge modification problems. A prominent example is Cluster Editing, where the objective is to partition the vertices into disjoint cliques. Although a \(2k\) kernel exists for this problem [Cao, 2012], it rarely reduces the size of practical instances. This leads us to examine whether linear kernels represent a theoretical barrier for edge modification problems, especially when the target graphs are highly structured—like a partition into cliques. To our knowledge, we establish the first sublinear kernel for an edge modification problem. Specifically, we demonstrate that Clique + Independent Set Deletion, a restricted form of Cluster Deletion, has a kernel of size \(O(k/\log k)\).

We also derive small kernels for several other edge modification problems. For Split Addition (and the equivalent Split Deletion), we present a linear kernel, improving on the quadratic kernel from Ghosh et al. [2015]. Additionally, we show that Trivially Perfect Addition admits a quadratic kernel, which improves the cubic kernel of Guo [2007]. Finally, we prove that its triangle-free variant, Starforest Deletion, has a linear kernel—a result that is optimal under the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH).

Stars appear in narrow, stream-like formations known as feathers, with widths of about 1–10 degrees and lengths reaching up to 180 degrees. These structures have been detected toward the Galactic Anticenter, and their characteristics have been mapped in terms of chemical abundances and phase-space. To investigate their origin, we analyze similar features produced in an N-body simulation of the Galactic disk interacting with a dwarf spheroidal galaxy resembling Sagittarius (Sgr). By tracing the past orbits of particles that form these feathers, we find their formation is linked to an earlier pericentric passage of Sgr. Initially, these particles occupy a broad range of phase-angles but a narrow range of radii, indicating they may serve as probes of distinct annular populations in the outer Galactic disk. The feather structures are long-lived, enduring over multiple passages for timescales of roughly 4 billion years. In the sky, they display oscillatory motion that can be described by a single orbit extending across much of their length, with amplitudes and gradients matching observations. We show how these feather properties can be used to measure the Galactic potential, its flattening, and to estimate the strength of recent gravitational disturbances.

The classification of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) into distinct groups has been widely explored through statistical methods. Past research has proposed the existence of an intermediate-duration group, separate from the established long and short bursts, though this category remains unconfirmed. Interestingly, satellite data from Swift and RHESSI present conflicting findings on the spectral hardness of these bursts. Analysis of the Swift/BAT database suggests that intermediate-duration bursts may be associated with X-ray flashes (XRFs). In contrast, data from RHESSI indicate these bursts are spectrally too hard to be classified as XRFs. The relationship between intermediate bursts and XRFs is also unclear in the BATSE database.

This study examines the connection between XRFs and GRBs using BATSE and RHESSI data, applying an empirical definition of XRFs established in earlier work. For RHESSI, a transformation between detected counts and fluences is performed based on simulated detector response functions, allowing comparison of burst hardness with the XRF definition.

Within the entire BATSE database, XRFs represent 1.3–4.2% of bursts. Most short BATSE bursts are not XRFs, with only 0.7–5.7% potentially attributable to them. The proportion of intermediate-duration bursts that could be XRFs is highly uncertain, ranging from 1% to 85%. For long bursts, the fraction lies between 1.0% and 3.4%. Despite substantial uncertainties, the data indicate that not all BATSE intermediate bursts can be explained as XRFs. In the RHESSI dataset, at least 79% of short bursts and at least 53% of intermediate-duration bursts…

Bivariate meta-analysis offers a valuable approach for synthesizing data from related studies, particularly in clinical research where treatment effects on two outcomes are evaluated together. This method has also been applied to examine surrogate relationships, where treatment effects on a surrogate endpoint are used to predict effects on a final clinical outcome. Surrogate endpoints are especially important in drug development, as they can provide earlier measures of treatment impact and help anticipate clinical benefits or risks.

The conventional bivariate meta-analysis models the observed treatment effects on both the surrogate and final outcomes using a bivariate normal distribution, accounting for variability within and between studies. For binomial data, a normal approximation of the log odds ratio is often employed. Yet, this approximation can introduce bias when event proportions are near zero or one, potentially compromising the validation of surrogate endpoints.

In this work, we investigate an alternative approach by modeling the two outcomes directly on the binomial scale. We first introduce a method that employs independent binomial likelihoods to capture within-study variability without approximating the observed treatment effects. While this avoids approximation issues, it does not consider within-study association. To address this limitation, we propose a second method using a bivariate copula with binomial marginals, which incorporates within-study correlation. Both methods are demonstrated through an example in chronic myeloid leukemia, exploring the surrogate relationship between complete cytogenetic response (CCyR) and event-free survival (EFS).

This paper outlines the fundamental concepts behind artificial photosynthesis for converting light into electrical energy. To illustrate these ideas, we analyze the function of the dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC)—a photoelectrochemical system that mimics the natural charge separation process across a nanostructured membrane. Such devices hold significant promise for competing with silicon-based technology in the photovoltaic market, particularly for applications where low cost and moderate efficiency are priorities.

Currently, ruthenium charge-transfer complexes serve as the light-absorbing elements in DSSCs. While they respond to a broad range of ultraviolet and visible light, they face challenges related to long-term stability and high production costs. We propose that melanins, a category of biological macromolecules, could effectively replace these ruthenium complexes. Melanins exhibit strong, wide-spectrum light absorption, high chemical and photochemical stability, and straightforward, low-cost synthesis. They are also bioavailable and biocompatible.

We present a regenerative solar cell based on melanin and examine the essential characteristics required for an efficient broadband light-harvesting system.

Observations using the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) will make it possible to study the majority of distant galaxies during the reionization epoch. Determining their properties—such as age, color excess E(B–V), specific star formation rate (sSFR), and stellar mass—will depend largely on spectral energy distribution fitting, using data from JWST’s two imaging cameras: the Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam) and the Mid Infrared Imager (MIRI). This study examines how the selection of different combinations of NIRCam and MIRI broad-band filters, covering 0.6 μm to 7.7 μm, affects the recovery of these galaxy properties.

Tests were conducted on a sample of 1542 simulated galaxies at redshifts z = 7–10, for which the input properties are known. Using only eight NIRCam broad bands, we recover the galaxy age within 0.1 Gyr and the color excess within 0.06 mag for 70% of the sample. Stellar masses and sSFR are recovered within 0.2 dex and 0.3 dex, respectively, at z = 7–9. At z = 10, however, no NIRCam band probes purely the λ > 4000 Å regime, leading to an increase in outliers for stellar mass (> 20%) and sSFR (> 90%) compared to z = 9. The MIRI F560W and F770W bands are essential for improving estimates of stellar mass and sSFR at z = 10.

When nebular emission lines are present, accurately determining galaxy properties becomes difficult at any redshift and with any filter combination. In particular, stellar mass

Machine learning often deals with data that lies on a low-dimensional manifold within a high-dimensional space. This high-dimensional nature makes many algorithms inefficient. A common remedy is to apply standard dimensionality reduction techniques like ISOMAP, Laplacian Eigenmaps, or LLE. Yet these methods often fail in practice because they assume nearly ideal data. Real-world datasets, however, frequently contain missing entries or excessive noise, making such algorithms unsuitable for direct use.

This paper addresses the specific challenge of missing data. While approaches like matrix completion can impute missing values, they typically overlook the nonlinear geometry of the underlying manifold. We introduce a new algorithm, MR-MISSING, which extends prior methods to compute low-dimensional representations from incomplete datasets. We validate its performance through three experiments: visually assessing results on synthetic manifolds, numerically comparing projections against those from nlPCA and mDRUR on the MNIST dataset, and demonstrating classification on MNIST with missing data. Additionally, we offer a theoretical guarantee for MR-MISSING under certain simplifying assumptions.

Ambient radio frequency signals are generated by diverse wireless networks worldwide. Through wireless information and power transfer, devices can simultaneously harvest energy from these signals and process information. This paper introduces a wireless information and power transfer protocol for two-way amplify-and-forward relaying channels, in which two sources communicate via an energy-harvesting relay. The relay extracts energy from incoming signals and applies it to power the transmission of forwarded signals. We derive exact analytical expressions for outage probability, ergodic capacity, and the finite-SNR diversity-multiplexing trade-off (DMT). Additionally, we establish tight closed-form upper and lower bounds for both outage probability and ergodic capacity. The influence of the power splitting ratio is also examined. Finally, we demonstrate that, compared to non-cooperative relaying, the proposed protocol offers a more sustainable approach, delivering higher transmission rates and greater reliability without requiring extra resources.

Graph representation learning has enhanced performance in tasks like link prediction and node classification across various domains. Studies indicate that numerous natural graphs exhibit hierarchical community structures, which has inspired methods leveraging these communities to refine node representations. Yet, such approaches often fail to use the learned representations to also enhance the quality of the discovered communities, missing an opportunity for iterative, joint optimization of representation learning and community discovery. Here, we introduce Mazi, an unsupervised algorithm that simultaneously learns the hierarchical community structure and node representations of a graph. To incorporate structural information, Mazi produces node representations at every level of the hierarchy and uses them to inform the representations in the original graph. Additionally, communities at each level are identified by concurrently maximizing modularity and minimizing the distance between a node’s representation and that of its community. We assess our method on a range of synthetic and real-world graphs through multi-label node classification and link prediction tasks, showing that Mazi surpasses both hierarchical and non-hierarchical alternatives.

We examine 26 Luminous Compact Blue Galaxies (LCBGs) in the Hubble Space Telescope’s Advanced Camera for Surveys Ultra Deep Field (UDF) at redshifts of approximately 0.2–1.3. The goal is to establish whether these objects are genuinely small galaxies or instead represent bright central starbursts within larger existing or forming disk galaxies. Surface brightness profiles derived from UDF imaging reach fainter than rest-frame 26.5 B mag/arcsec², even for compact sources at z~1.

Most LCBGs display two components: a smaller, brighter region likely undergoing star formation, and a more extended, roughly exponential component whose colors indicate stellar ages from about 100 Myr to a few gigayears. The scale lengths of these extended components are generally greater than about 2 kpc, which is 1.5–2 times smaller than those of nearby large disk galaxies such as the Milky Way. We can rule out the presence of larger, very low surface brightness disks down to faint rest-frame limits (approximately 26 B mag/arcsec²).

Nevertheless, one or two LCBGs are larger, disk-like galaxies that satisfy LCBG selection criteria because of a bright central nucleus—possibly a bulge in formation. These findings suggest that more than about 90% of high-redshift LCBGs are small galaxies that will passively evolve into small disk galaxies or low-mass spheroidal or irregular galaxies in the local universe, assuming no substantial later disk growth. The data show no evidence of disk formation around compact, HII-galaxy-like LCBGs, nor do they support a simple inside-out growth model for the larger LCBGs with disk-like morphology. In distant LCBGs, irregular blue emission is relatively extended, implying that nebular emission lines from star-forming regions trace a significant portion of each galaxy’s velocity field

We use the Gamow shell model (GSM) to study low-lying states in the oxygen isotopes 24O and 25O. The many-body Schrödinger equation is solved using a two-body Hamiltonian based on a renormalized low-momentum nucleon-nucleon interaction within a spherical Berggren basis. This basis treats bound, resonant, and continuum states equally, making it well suited for describing loosely bound and unbound nuclear states near threshold. Our results indicate that such a basis is essential for accurately describing the low-lying 1+ and 2+ excited states in 24O. In contrast, reproducing the binding energy systematics of the ground states requires careful treatment and inclusion of many-body correlations. This is reinforced by our finding that 25O is unstable relative to 24O in both oscillator and Berggren representations when starting from a 22O core. Additionally, we demonstrate that the structure of these weakly bound or unbound isotopes is significantly affected by the 1S0 component of the nucleon-nucleon interaction, which carries important implications for understanding nuclear stability.

We present a method for blind signal recovery or blind mixture identification in scenarios where such tasks were previously considered unfeasible. These include: (i) closely spaced or strongly correlated sources in antenna array processing, (ii) highly correlated spreading codes in CDMA radio communication, and (iii) nearly dependent spectra in fluorescent spectroscopy. This advancement carries significant practical consequences. For antenna arrays, it enables the deterministic joint localization and extraction of multiple sources from noisy multi-sensor measurements. In CDMA systems, it permits accommodating more users than the spreading gain would typically allow. In fluorescent spectroscopy, it makes possible the detection of chemical constituents with nearly identical spectral signatures.

The method is based on solving a bounded coherence low-rank multilinear approximation problem. We demonstrate that bounded coherence ensures the existence and uniqueness of the recovered solution. Statistical motivation for the approximation problem is provided, along with a discussion of greedy approximation bounds. To establish a theoretical foundation, we develop a corresponding framework for sparse separable decompositions of functions. This includes generalized notions of rank and nuclear norm, which extend conventional matrix and operator concepts to hypermatrices and tensors.

Understanding the severity of an influenza outbreak is essential for guiding and evaluating public health measures during and after an epidemic. Yet directly measuring risks such as fatality, intensive care admission, and hospitalization among cases remains challenging. In earlier work, Bayesian evidence synthesis methods were used to integrate incomplete, underreported, and biased surveillance data into a coherent framework, allowing estimation of case-severity risks during the first two waves of the 2009 A/H1N1 pandemic in England. Here, we describe in detail the complex probabilistic model that supports this evidence synthesis and expand the analysis to estimate severity in a third wave that occurred during the 2010/2011 influenza season. The model is adjusted to reflect changes in the surveillance data available across all three waves. We compare two strategies: (a) a two-stage approach, where posterior distributions from the model for the first two waves inform prior distributions for the third wave; and (b) a one-stage approach that models all three waves together. Both methods lead to the same main findings: (1) case-severity risks follow a U-shaped age pattern, with the highest severity among children and older adults; (2) the age distribution of the infection attack rate shifts across waves—school-age children were most affected in the first two waves, while adults over 25 saw an increase from the second to third wave; and (3) overall case-severity, averaged across age groups, rose over the course of the three waves. Whether the last finding is primarily due to shifts in the age profile of infections over time remains open to discussion.

The High Altitude Water Cherenkov (HAWC) observatory consists of an array of large water Cherenkov detectors designed to detect gamma rays and hadronic cosmic rays with energies ranging from 100 GeV to 100 TeV. By observing the secondary particles that reach the ground when primary particles interact in the atmosphere above it, HAWC will measure high-energy protons and cosmic rays. Located on the northern slope of the Sierra Negra volcano in central Mexico at 19 degrees north latitude and 4,100 meters above sea level, the observatory is under construction and slated for completion in 2014. This paper presents sensitivity estimates for HAWC in detecting both point-like and extended gamma-ray sources, modeling source fluxes with unbroken power laws as well as power laws featuring exponential cutoffs. Over one year, HAWC is projected to be sensitive to point sources with integral power-law spectra as low as 5×10⁻¹³ cm⁻² s⁻¹ above 2 TeV (about 50 mCrab) across 5 steradians of sky. This conservative estimate, based on basic event parameters, is anticipated to improve with refined data analysis methods. We also examine known TeV sources and the potential scientific insights HAWC could provide into particle acceleration mechanisms within these sources.

Choosing relevant features for prediction or classification tasks is a key challenge in fields that work with high-dimensional data. Feature selection helps mitigate the curse of dimensionality, enhances the performance of predictive models, and supports clearer interpretation of results. In nonlinear settings, mutual information is commonly adopted as a criterion to evaluate the relevance of individual features or feature sets. However, this approach faces at least three significant drawbacks: mutual information estimators rely on smoothing parameters, there is no theoretically grounded stopping rule for greedy feature selection algorithms, and the estimation process itself is vulnerable to the curse of dimensionality. This chapter presents methods to overcome these issues. The first two are tackled through resampling techniques, which offer a statistical foundation for choosing estimator parameters and determining when to halt the search. The third limitation is addressed by refining the mutual information criterion into a measure that captures how features complement one another—not just how informative they are individually—for the specific problem.

According to the sociological concept of homophily, individuals with shared characteristics tend to engage more frequently with each other. Marketers can use data on customer interactions, guided by this principle, to infer hidden similarities. Yet, as networks grow, the number of potential interactions increases quadratically, making it impractical to model social connections with probability-based methods except in very small networks. This paper introduces a probabilistic model for customer interactions that incorporates homophily while allowing for randomness in interaction patterns. We propose a new Bayesian nonparametric method based on Dirichlet processes to address the scalability challenges common in marketing research with network data. This approach proves effective for uncovering latent customer similarities, and we explore its practical applications for customer segmentation and targeted marketing.

This research investigates the propagation of charm quarks during the early phase of high-energy proton-lead collisions, modeling their interactions with the evolving Glasma via the Wong equations. At the initial time, quantum fluctuations are neglected, and the Glasma consists of longitudinal fields; however, the dynamics rapidly generate transverse fields. We estimate this formation time as Δt ≈ 0.1 fm/c, which is comparable to the formation time of heavy quark pairs, t_formation ≈ 1/(2m). Using a simplified static longitudinal geometry, we observe that strong transverse color fields accelerate heavy quarks in the early stage, tilting the c-quark spectrum toward higher p_T states. This average acceleration can be interpreted as drag and diffusion of c-quarks in a hot medium, analogous to the acceleration of electrons emitted from an electron cannon in a cathode tube—an effect we term the cathode tube effect. The spectral tilt influences the nuclear modification factor, R_pPb, suppressing it below unity at low p_T and raising it above unity at intermediate p_T. After evolving the charm quarks in the gluon fields, we calculate R_pPb(p_T) and find its shape qualitatively matches measurements of the same quantity for D-mesons in proton-lead collisions.

Computers now play a growing role in making decisions that profoundly affect people’s lives. These predictive systems can often impact various population subgroups unevenly, which has spurred rising concern around fairness. In response, researchers have proposed numerous classifiers and predictors designed to improve fairness. This paper investigates how these different methods fundamentally relate to each other and what drives their differences. In particular, we highlight several overlooked dimensions of such fairness-enhancing approaches. We introduce an open benchmark that enables comparison across multiple algorithms, fairness metrics, and a wide range of existing datasets. Our findings show that while algorithms often align with specific fairness definitions, many fairness measures are strongly correlated. We also observe that fairness-preserving methods are frequently sensitive to changes in dataset composition—simulated here by varying training-test splits—suggesting these interventions may be less robust than earlier believed.

Creating an efficient silicon-based light source remains a key challenge in photonics, as silicon's indirect bandgap inhibits direct carrier recombination and photon emission. In this work, we demonstrate an electrically driven light-emitting silicon tunnel junction that operates at room temperature by harnessing inelastically tunneling electrons. This junction generates plasmons powered by the electrical tunnel current. Contrary to the quantum condition where emission frequency scales with applied voltage, the emission spectrum arises from the spectral overlap between the energy-dependent tunnel current and the plasmon's modal dispersion. Experimentally, the highest light outcoupling efficiency aligns with the skin depth of the metallic contact in this metal-insulator-semiconductor structure. Unlike LEDs, whose temporal response is limited by nanosecond carrier lifetimes in semiconductors, this tunnel source is governed by the tunneling event and the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.

In dense millimeter wave (mmWave) networks, user association is required to decide which base station each user should connect to, balancing loads across base stations and maximizing overall throughput. Because mmWave links are highly directional and sensitive to minor channel fluctuations, changes in user association directly alter these connections, substantially impacting both a user’s instantaneous data rate and network-wide interference. This paper presents a new load balancing user association method for mmWave MIMO networks that accounts for how user transmission rates and interference depend on association choices. We model the user association challenge as a mixed integer nonlinear programming problem and develop a polynomial-time algorithm—Worst Connection Swapping (WCS)—to obtain a near-optimal solution. Simulations show that the proposed approach greatly enhances network performance by shifting traffic from congested base stations to less loaded ones and correspondingly adjusting interference. Moreover, the WCS algorithm surpasses other general combinatorial programming methods, such as genetic algorithms, delivering higher accuracy and running orders of magnitude faster. In small networks where exhaustive search is feasible, WCS achieves the optimal solution.

Our findings reveal that the Hawking radiation from a large Schwarzschild black hole differs substantially from a classical state, despite its seemingly thermal character. In this state, individual modes of massless asymptotic fields—such as photons, gravitons, and potentially neutrinos—have low occupation numbers, leading to significant relative fluctuations. Occupation numbers for massive fields are even smaller, suppressed beyond the usual Boltzmann factor. Consequently, this thermal state cannot be considered classical or semiclassical.

We support this conclusion by demonstrating that physical observables in a low-occupation state exhibit large quantum fluctuations, which cannot be accurately captured by mean-field approximations or WKB-like semiclassical descriptions. Given the unitary evolution of the black hole, these results indicate that the interior state, when expressed in terms of asymptotic fields, must also be non-classical. Moreover, such a non-classical interior cannot be represented by a semiclassical geometry, even though the average curvature remains below the Planck scale.

Bayesian optimization (BO) is a popular approach for solving optimization problems where evaluating the objective function is costly. Typically, BO methods rely on modeling the objective with a Gaussian Process (GP), choosing its covariance from a family of standard covariance functions. From the weight-space perspective, this represents the objective as a linear function in a feature space defined by the chosen covariance K, assuming a Gaussian prior on the weights, ${\bf w} \sim \mathcal{N} ({\bf 0}, {\bf I})$. In real-world scenarios, auxiliary data often exists that shares a similar covariance structure with the objective, yet differs in form, preventing its direct use in conventional transfer learning. This paper introduces a method to leverage such auxiliary data to design a GP covariance that reflects a more suitable weight prior for the objective. Based on this, we illustrate how BO can be accelerated by employing this learned weight prior to model the objective, validating the approach with both benchmark test functions and a real-world application in short-polymer fiber manufacturing.

Leveraging the vast amount of general-domain dialogue data available, pre-trained dialogue generation offers a promising approach to transfer knowledge from broad sources to specific downstream applications. Typically, this transferable capability is achieved by training large models with hundreds of millions of parameters on extensive datasets, a process that is often computationally intensive and lacks interpretability. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel dialogue generation model featuring a latent structure designed for efficient and transparent transfer from general domains to targeted tasks. Evaluations on two benchmark datasets confirm the model’s effectiveness. By utilizing this transferable latent structure, our approach generates higher-quality dialogue responses than four strong baseline models, as measured by both automated metrics and human assessment. Notably, with only about 22% of the parameters, our model achieves a fivefold increase in processing speed compared to the strongest baseline. Additionally, the model provides explainability through the interpretation of its discrete latent variables.

Measurements of hadronic event shape distributions in e+e- annihilation, conducted by the OPAL experiment at center-of-mass energies from 91 GeV to 209 GeV, are employed to extract the strong coupling constant αS. The analysis uses QCD predictions calculated to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) and combines these with resummed next-to-leading-log-approximation terms (NNLO+NLLA). From all observables and energies, the combined NNLO result yields αS(mZ0) = 0.1201 ± 0.0008 (stat.) ± 0.0013 (exp.) ± 0.0010 (had.) ± 0.0024 (theo.), while the combined NNLO+NLLA result gives αS(mZ0) = 0.1189 ± 0.0008 (stat.) ± 0.0016 (exp.) ± 0.0010 (had.) ± 0.0036 (theo.). Compared to earlier studies based only on NLO or NLO+NLLA predictions, the NNLO and NNLO+NLLA results show greater completeness with respect to missing higher-order contributions, as assessed through renormalization scale variations. The measured energy dependence of αS is consistent with the QCD expectation of asymptotic freedom and rules out a non-running coupling.

This article introduces the "multiplier algebra" for the Fock representation derived from a left-cancellative semigroup $\mathcal{S}$, denoted $M(\mathcal{S})$, by extending the notion of the multiplier algebra of a $C^*$-algebra. We examine fundamental properties and examples of such algebras. To properly define the multiplier algebra, two essential results are established. It is shown that $M(\mathcal{S})$ forms a unital Banach algebra whenever $\mathcal{S}$ is left-cancellative. In the case where $\mathcal{S}$ is a group $G$, we prove that $M(G)$ is a $C^*$-algebra.

We identify the multiplier algebras $M(\mathbb{Z}_{+})$ and $M(\mathbb{Z}^2_{+})$ with the corresponding Hardy algebras $H^{\infty}(\mathbb{D})$ and $H^{\infty}(\mathbb{D}^2)$ for $\mathcal{S}=\mathbb{Z}_{+}$ and $\mathbb{Z}^2_{+}$, respectively. The multiplier algebra associated with the free semigroup $\mathcal{S}=\mathbb{F}^+_{n}$ is also examined. Here, we clearly establish an isometric isomorphism between the well-known non-commutative Hardy algebra $\mathbb{F}_{n}^{\infty}$—introduced and extensively studied by G. Popescu—and the multiplier algebra $M(\mathbb{F}_{n}^+)$.

Finally, employing operator space methods, we demonstrate a notable result: $M(\mathcal{S})$ is an operator algebra, a conclusion supported by the Blecher–Ruan–Sinclair theorem.

A widely studied approach to modeling the quantum evolution of the early universe, aside from including a cosmological constant, uses the most general action compatible with an isotropic and homogeneous spacetime. The presence of curvature-squared terms ensures the effective Hamiltonian operator remains Hermitian, which guarantees unitary time evolution and preserves probability. Oscillations in the semi-classical wavefunction around a de-Sitter solution indicate that the theory is classically permissible, leading the universe into an inflationary phase shortly after the Planck epoch. By employing a hierarchy of Hubble flow parameters and redefining the effective potential, the complex classical field equations—which involve multiple coupling parameters—simplify to standard general-relativistic equations with a single scalar field. This allows inflation to be analyzed without introducing extra flow parameters. The resulting inflationary predictions align well with current Planck data, and the model supports a graceful exit from inflation.

Compressible magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) turbulence is common across astrophysical systems, from intergalactic to stellar scales. Because of the strong nonlinearities in these systems, numerical simulations are often essential. Yet the vast dynamical ranges involved exceed current computational limits. In large eddy simulations (LES), the effects of limited resolution are handled by adding terms to the equations of motion that represent unresolved subgrid-scale (SGS) dynamics, which leaves the system mathematically unclosed. This work develops a set of nonlinear structural closures for the ideal MHD LES equations, focusing especially on compressibility effects. The closures are built from a gradient expansion of the finite-resolution operator (W.K. Yeo CUP 1993, ed. Galperin & Orszag) and do not assume any specific properties of the flow or magnetic field. As a result, they can be applied across regimes—from subsonic to hypersonic and from sub-Alfvénic to hyper-Alfvénic. These closures allow spectral energy to cascade both up and down in scale, and they enable direct energy transfer between kinetic and magnetic components, both resolved and unresolved. They also naturally account for local flow geometry, including anisotropy. The closures are validated *a priori* in a companion paper (Grete et al. Phys. Plasmas, 2016) by comparing them with other available closures using a broad set of simulation data from homogeneous, isotropic turbulence.

This study examines the physical nature of polar jets in the solar corona and their potential role in coronal heating and solar wind generation, using X-ray images from the Hinode XRT telescope. By analyzing two observational datasets focused on polar coronal holes, acquired with a sequence of three XRT filters, we assess the energy forms—especially kinetic energy and enthalpy—associated with numerous small-scale eruptions. From the observed sequences, typical kinematic properties (such as length, thickness, lifetime, ejection rate, and velocity) are evaluated for 18 jets, offering insight into their possible contribution to the fast solar wind flux from coronal holes. Electron temperatures and densities of the jet plasmas are also derived from intensity ratios across different filters.

The findings indicate that thermal energy constitutes the largest portion of energy supplied to the corona. Wave energy may also be substantial, though its magnitude remains relatively uncertain. Kinetic energy is lower than thermal energy, while other energy forms are comparatively minor. Smaller and fainter events appear to be hotter, implying that the overall contribution of polar jets to coronal heating may have been underestimated. The kinetic energy flux is typically about three times smaller than the enthalpy flux, meaning more energy goes into heating the plasma than accelerating it. This suggests that most polar jets likely do not escape the Sun, and only cooler ejections may possess sufficient kinetic energy to contribute meaningfully to the solar wind.

We present a straightforward magneto-optical experiment and a corresponding magneto-optical Kramers-Kronig analysis (MOKKA), which together enable the determination of the complex dielectric function for left- and right-handed circular polarizations across a wide frequency range—without the need to produce circularly polarized light. The method involves measuring either reflectivity and Kerr rotation, or transmission and Faraday rotation, at normal incidence using ordinary broadband polarizers and without any retarders or quarter-wave plates. When magneto-optical rotation is small (typically below ∼0.2 rad), a rapid measurement approach can be used in which the polarizers remain fixed at a 45° relative angle. This protocol is not only time-efficient but also advantageous in challenging environments, such as ultra-high magnetic fields, where rotating polarizers in situ is impractical. The technique can be viewed as a magneto-optical extension of the standard Kramers-Kronig analysis of reflectivity in bulk samples, as well as of Kramers-Kronig constrained variational analysis applied to more intricate spectral data. We illustrate the method using the classic semimetals bismuth and graphite, and further apply it to extract handedness-resolved magneto-absorption spectra of graphene on SiC.

Many environmental phenomena involve multiple correlated variables that must be examined together to accurately represent their nature. For such cases, identifying extremes through marginal analysis alone is inadequate. Traditionally, extremes have been associated with quantiles, a key tool for risk assessment in single-variable settings. In this work, we propose using the directional multivariate quantile to identify multivariate extremes and analyze environmental events. This approach accounts for all relevant variables and examines the data along meaningful directions that can better characterize environmental disasters. While copula models are widely used in the literature for detecting extremes, we extend the copula method by incorporating a directional perspective. The paper discusses the advantages and limitations of both our non-parametric approach and existing copula techniques. Using simulated and real datasets, we demonstrate how focusing on the first principal component direction enhances the visualization of extremes. Two case studies are presented: a synthetic example of flood risk at a dam (involving three variables) and a real-world analysis of sea storms (involving five variables).

We present predictions for the nuclear modification factor \( R_{pA} \) for pions and direct photons in proton–nucleus collisions at LHC energies at midrapidity. These are obtained using several saturation models, each calibrated to HERA data. Our framework contains no free parameters, as all model inputs are determined from independent reactions. The results describe PHENIX data on \( R_{pA} \) for pions reasonably well.

In the saturation models considered, gluon shadowing at LHC midrapidity replaces the pion Cronin enhancement seen at lower energies with a modest suppression. In contrast, direct photons can retain a Cronin-type enhancement at the LHC in models with a higher saturation scale. Both shadowing and saturation effects are significant in LHC proton–nucleus collisions and produce a substantial impact on \( R_{pA} \). Consequently, precise measurements of proton–nucleus collisions at the LHC are essential for clarifying the dynamics that govern heavy-ion collisions.

Methods like wav2vec2 and w2v-BERT use masked speech modeling (MSM) to learn representations from randomly masked speech frames in an utterance. Although these approaches enhance Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) performance, they share a key drawback: treating all unsupervised speech samples equally, even though not every sample contains useful information for learning meaningful representations. To overcome this limitation, we introduce ask2mask (ATM), a novel method that prioritizes specific samples during MSM pre-training. ATM uses an external ASR model, or scorer, to weight unsupervised input samples in two distinct manners. First, it performs fine-grained data selection by masking only the input frames with high confidence according to the scorer, enabling the model to learn more meaningful representations. Second, ATM extends this focus to the utterance level by weighting the final MSM loss with an utterance-level confidence score. We evaluate ATM through fine-tuning experiments on two well-established corpora: LibriSpeech (which matches the pre-training data) and a combination of CommonVoice, TED-LIUM, AMI, and CHiME-6 (which do not match the pre-training data). The results confirm ATM’s effectiveness, showing substantial recognition improvements in mismatched conditions—up to 11.6% relative improvement over published results and up to 4.46% relative over our internal baseline—while still delivering modest gains in matched conditions.

Effective collective navigation for biological or artificial agents must balance competing demands: maintaining group cohesion while preventing collisions, all with minimal energy spent on maneuvering. We explore this challenge using a system of active Brownian particles confined to a two-dimensional area, seeking the control strategy that best reconciles collision avoidance with control effort. Framing the problem within optimal stochastic control theory and applying a mean-field game approach, we obtain an analytical mean-field solution that exhibits a second-order phase transition in the alignment order parameter. Interestingly, a mean-field adaptation of the classic Vicsek model—which relies on alignment interactions—performs nearly as well as the optimal control. These findings support the idea that observed group behaviors can arise from optimizing multiple objectives and provide a theoretical foundation for biomimetic algorithms in artificial agent systems.

In recent years, scientific research has grown increasingly collaborative, driven by a rise in multi-author papers and the growth of interdisciplinary work involving specialists from diverse fields. In this context, measuring the individual impact of researchers presents a challenge. This study examines how a researcher’s most frequent collaboration—measured by co-authored works—affects their productivity and visibility metrics. Specifically, we assess how an author’s output, citations, and h-index ranking change when publications co-authored with their most prolific collaborator are excluded. Our analysis reveals distinct patterns of collaborator influence across major fields of knowledge. In formal and applied sciences, for example, prolific collaborators appear to significantly affect an author’s visibility metrics, even among highly cited researchers. These findings can help stakeholders better understand collaboration dynamics and develop success measures that account for collaborative relationships.

To grasp the current state of research on dark matter, one must first trace its historical context—only then do the scattered pieces of the puzzle align. This brief review not only clarifies that history but also uncovers a significant and intriguing issue: the diminished role of electromagnetism in modern astronomy. After the early 20th century, interest in electromagnetic influences seemed to fade, leaving gravitational effects as the primary focus in explaining cosmic phenomena. In light of this, the discussion shifts toward plasma physics, exploring whether its principles might offer a more plausible explanation for the missing mass problem. Although the main aim here is to address dark matter and why it has been proposed, this renewed attention to electromagnetism—coupled with the recognition that most cosmic matter exists as plasma—also points to fresh avenues for solving other persistent mysteries in astronomy.

If the universe's accelerating expansion results from a late-time modification of general relativity, the growth of large-scale structure should also diverge from standard cosmological predictions. We use the statistical distribution of galaxy cluster-sized halos to test theories of gravity. Using multiple matched N-body simulations with identical initial conditions and expansion histories, we compare DGP and f(R) gravity models across various parameters. From each simulation, we derive the cluster mass function, power spectrum, and mean pairwise velocity at redshifts 1, 0.3, and 0. All three statistics show systematic variations between gravity models. The mean pairwise velocity offers a key consistency check for any proposed deviation from general relativity indicated by measurements of the power spectrum and cluster mass function. Future microwave background experiments, such as Simons Observatory and CMB-S4, will detect tens of thousands of galaxy clusters through the thermal Sunyaev-Zeldovich effect, estimate their masses via microwave background lensing, and possibly measure velocities using transverse lensing or the kinematic Sunyaev-Zeldovich effect. These cluster observations are poised to become a powerful tool for investigating modified gravity.

Consider the model \( y = A\beta + \epsilon \), where \( y \) is an \( N \times 1 \) vector of observations, \( \beta \) is a \( p \times 1 \) vector of unknown regression coefficients, \( A \) is an \( N \times p \) design matrix, and \( \epsilon \) is a spherically symmetric error term with an unknown scale parameter \( \sigma \). This work focuses on estimating \( \beta \) under general quadratic loss functions. It extends earlier results by Strawderman and Casella by identifying adaptive minimax estimators for \( \beta \) that, under normality assumptions, are also generalized Bayes. These estimators offer improved numerical stability—specifically, a smaller condition number—compared to the standard least squares estimator. Notably, we present a subclass of these estimators that takes a remarkably simple form. Additionally, we demonstrate that, under certain conditions, the generalized Bayes minimax estimators derived under normality remain generalized Bayes and minimax in the broader setting of spherically symmetric errors.

Recent advances in quantum hardware are opening new possibilities for its application across various fields. Yet the engineering of quantum software remains at an early stage, facing significant hurdles—particularly the wide array of quantum programming languages and hardware platforms. To address these issues, we introduce QFaaS, a new Quantum Function-as-a-Service framework that applies serverless computing benefits and modern software engineering methods to make quantum computing more practical. This framework offers core elements of a quantum serverless platform, streamlining software development and aligning with the quantum cloud computing model through features like hybrid quantum-classical computation, function containerization, and integrated DevOps tools. QFaaS is designed as a unified framework that supports leading quantum languages and software development kits—including Qiskit, Q#, Cirq, and Braket—and runs quantum tasks across multiple simulators and cloud providers such as IBM Quantum and Amazon Braket. In this paper, we describe the architecture, key components, lifecycle of hybrid quantum-classical functions, operational workflow, and implementation of QFaaS. We also illustrate two real-world use cases and evaluate performance on quantum computers and simulators, showing how the framework reduces complexity for conventional engineers and helps accelerate the shift toward quantum software.

Density estimation is a well-established statistical challenge, important both for fully observed data and for partially observed or censored samples. In fields like survival analysis and clinical trials, data collection often involves right-censored observations, where random events may prevent complete measurement of the variable of interest. This makes it valuable to develop reliable methods for estimating lifetimes under such conditions.

This paper introduces a nonparametric linear density estimator built from fully data-driven empirical wavelet coefficients. Using an inductive bias correction procedure, we construct an asymptotically unbiased estimator from the complete sample. We also establish upper bounds for the bias and examine the large-sample behavior of the expected $\mathbb{L}_{2}$ estimation error, following the approach of Stute (1995). The results show that the estimates are asymptotically normal and globally mean-square consistent.

We further assess the method through a theoretical simulation study, testing various baseline distributions across different sample sizes. The censoring scheme applied yields an average censoring proportion of 40%. Finally, the estimator is applied to previously published real-world datasets, demonstrating that this wavelet-based approach offers a robust and practical tool for nonparametric estimation of the survival time density function.

LUSTRE is a synchronous reactive dataflow language designed for modeling, simulating, and verifying safety-critical systems. Its toolset supports these tasks across a broad range of applications. A key stage in the formally verified compilation of LUSTRE is its translation into a restricted, well-behaved subset known as Normalised LUSTRE (NLUSTRE). In prior work, we introduced a straightforward Denning-style lattice-based type system for enforcing secure information flow in NLUSTRE and demonstrated its soundness by proving that well-typed programs satisfy non-interference under co-inductive stream semantics.

This paper extends that approach by presenting a comparable security type system for full LUSTRE. We demonstrate that the semantics-preserving normalization transformations from LUSTRE to NLUSTRE, as defined by Bourke et al., also preserve security guarantees. A key innovation is the introduction of refinement security types for node calls. Our central contribution is proving that these normalization transformations maintain security typing. Soundness of the type system is established by showing that well-security-typed programs are non-interfering. This result follows from combining type preservation (shown here), semantics preservation (from Bourke et al.), and our earlier non-interference proof for NLUSTRE.

We examine a basic lattice model that gives rise to a gauge theory. This model involves the interaction of a gauge field with a complex matrix scalar field of size \(N \times N\), which transforms in the fundamental representation. Unlike the Kazakov-Migdal model, it includes only a linear coupling between the scalar and gauge fields on the lattice and avoids additional local U(1) symmetries.

Within an approximation based on a translation-invariant master field, the large \(N\) limit of the model is analyzed. In this limit, the gauge fields can be integrated out, producing an effective theory that describes interactions among the eigenvalues of the master field. The resulting reduced model shows phase transitions at the critical points \(\beta_{\bar{cr}}\) and \(\beta_{\underline{cr}}\). The interval \((\beta_{\bar{cr}}, \beta_{\underline{cr}})\) marks the boundary between the strong and weak coupling regimes of the model.

To investigate the large \(N\) behavior more systematically, we apply the quenched momentum prescription with constraints, a method used to handle the large \(N\) limit in gauge theories. Using orthogonal polynomial techniques, we derive nonlinear equations that characterize the large \(N\) limit of the reduced model under quenching.

A novel technique from nonlinear dynamical systems is applied to assess Lagrangian connectivity in the deep global ocean. Using Argo float trajectories from January 2011 to January 2017, we compute the dynamic Laplacian and identify the eight most prominent coherent regions—areas that remain dynamically self-connected—at a depth of 1500 meters. This method addresses challenges such as sparse observational data and the continuous turnover of floats in the dataset; only 10% of floats provided continuous measurements over the entire six-year period. The detected coherent regions effectively retain water within their boundaries throughout the six-year analysis, offering a unique perspective on deep-ocean dynamics and valuable insights for planning future float deployments. Although this study focuses on multi-year, global-scale ocean circulation, the dynamic Laplacian approach can be adapted to any temporal or spatial scale to reveal coherent structures in ocean flows, using positional time series data from observations or models.

As generative adversarial networks (GANs) progress quickly, the visual realism of synthesized scenes continues to rise—even for intricate urban environments used in automated driving. This work focuses on continual scene generation, where GANs are trained sequentially on a series of distinct domains. The goal is for the models to ultimately produce new scenes across all previously encountered domains, mirroring real-world situations in which data are gathered continuously across various locations and times. In such a continual learning setting, we target zero forgetting—that is, preventing any decline in synthesis quality for earlier domains due to catastrophic forgetting. We propose a new framework that both (i) supports smooth knowledge transfer during continual training and (ii) ensures zero forgetting with minimal additional overhead. More memory-efficient through continual learning, our model achieves higher synthesis quality than a brute-force approach that trains a separate full model per domain. Notably, in extremely low-data conditions, our method significantly outperforms the brute-force alternative.

We present an exact infinite power series that fully describes the librating and rotating motion of a nonlinear simple pendulum for all time. While the series coefficients were previously reported in the literature, the series itself—along with the optimal expansion point needed to ensure convergence and maximize its domain—was not previously analyzed and is introduced here. Represented as an elliptic function, the pendulum’s solution possesses singularities located off the real axis in the complex time plane, which imposes a finite radius of convergence on the physical problem in real time. Expanding about the top of the trajectory allows the power series to converge all the way to the bottom without interference from these singularities. In deriving the series, we obtain the coefficients through an alternative method that can be extended to other nonlinear problems in mathematical physics. Furthermore, we develop an exact resummation of the pendulum series—inspired by the asymptotic approximant technique—which uniformly accelerates convergence from the top to the bottom of the trajectory. We also provide an accelerated exact resummation of the infinite series for the elliptic integral used in computing the pendulum’s period. This preserves analyticity when employing the period to extend the pendulum series for all time via symmetry.

The "raspberry" model is a hybrid lattice-Boltzmann and Langevin molecular dynamics approach for simulating colloidal suspensions, first introduced by V. Lobaskin and B. Dünweg [New J. Phys. 6, 54 (2004)]. It employs discrete surface points to couple fluid and particle dynamics. This paper extends our earlier investigation of the model’s ability to capture hydrodynamic interactions in the Stokes regime for spheres in a simple-cubic crystal [L. Fischer et al., J. Chem. Phys. 143, 084108 (2015)]. Here, we evaluate its accuracy for particles confined between two parallel plates. Our lattice-Boltzmann simulation results are compared with established theoretical predictions and finite-element calculations. As in Part I of our joint publication, we observe a mismatch between translational and rotational mobility when only surface coupling points are used. We further show that incorporating internal coupling points into the raspberry model corrects this discrepancy in confined geometries. Finally, we demonstrate that the model reliably reproduces hydrodynamic interactions between a spherical colloid and planar walls down to approximately one lattice spacing in the lattice-Boltzmann framework.

Wireless energy transfer (WET) using radio-frequency (RF) signals has attracted increasing attention as a viable way to deliver cost-effective and reliable power to energy-limited wireless networks. A key technique for making WET feasible is energy beamforming, which uses multiple antennas at the energy transmitter (ET) to focus wireless energy more efficiently toward intended energy receivers (ERs) and overcome substantial propagation losses over distance. The effectiveness of energy beamforming, however, heavily relies on the channel state information (CSI) available at the ET, which must be obtained in practice.

This paper investigates an efficient channel acquisition method for a point-to-point multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) WET system by taking advantage of channel reciprocity—here, the ET estimates CSI through dedicated reverse-link training from the ER. Given the ER’s limited energy, the training strategy must balance accurate channel estimation with minimal energy expenditure at the ER. To address this, we propose maximizing the net harvested energy at the ER, defined as the average harvested energy minus the energy used for channel training. We formulate an optimization problem for training design over MIMO Rician fading channels, covering decisions such as which ER antennas to train, along with allocated training time and power. Closed-form solutions are derived for certain special cases, offering insight into when training should be applied to enhance net transferred energy in MIMO WET systems.

We demonstrate that any soliton in a two-dimensional integrable system can eventually evaporate, emitting an exact analog of Hawking radiation from black holes. Using the AKNS matrix framework for integrability, we associate a real spacetime metric tensor with the soliton, which describes a curved surface experienced by classical and quantum fluctuations propagating on it. By constructing appropriate scalar invariants of the corresponding Riemannian geometry and incorporating the conformal anomaly, we compute the Hawking temperatures and entropies for fundamental solitons of the nonlinear Schrödinger, KdV, and sine-Gordon equations. This proposed mechanism is straightforward, universally applicable to all two-dimensional integrable equations, and readily extends to a broad range of black holes in any number of dimensions. It thereby reveals entirely new perspectives on soliton quantum mechanics and their profound links to black hole physics.

We study the photoluminescence, dynamics, and photon statistics of exciton complexes in a site-controlled system of inverted pyramidal InGaAs/GaAs quantum dots embedded in photonic crystal cavities. In this platform, we observe a concurrent strong–weak coupling regime, where the typical sharp distinction between strong and weak quantum dot–cavity coupling is replaced by a dynamic coexistence mediated by phonon scattering.

Microphotoluminescence spectra as a function of detuning show several simultaneous features: an avoided crossing of the exciton–cavity polariton mode, characteristic of strong coupling and Rabi splitting; a blue shift of the coupled exciton–cavity mode near zero detuning, attributed to phonon-assisted coupling and the formation of collective states; and a partial linewidth narrowing associated with the mixed behavior of the modes.

By analyzing the optical properties of strongly confined exciton–photon complexes and the quantum statistics of cavity photons, we uncover the dynamics and photon antibunching/bunching signatures of this intermediate strong–weak coupled system near zero detuning. These results indicate that our device could enable new cavity quantum electrodynamic effects, cavity-enhanced generation of indistinguishable single photons, and the formation of cluster states via exciton–photon complexes for quantum networks.

We investigate how electrical resistivity varies with temperature in interacting two-dimensional metals. Using semiclassical Boltzmann transport theory, we numerically simulate nonequilibrium states. Our simulations show that deviations from standard Fermi-liquid predictions can occur because of the particular scattering geometry of umklapp processes—in certain cases, even at ultra-low temperatures. Umklapp scattering is necessary to relax the total momentum of the quasiparticle distribution. We examine transport in a two-dimensional quasiparticle system with repulsive on-site interactions and nonmagnetic impurity scattering, modeled on a square lattice with a single-orbital tight-binding dispersion. Our results indicate that unconventional scaling of electrical resistivity—often taken as a sign of a non-Fermi-liquid state—can emerge from specific geometric features of the Fermi surface. The persistence of these deviations from Fermi-liquid theory in our straightforward model offers a new perspective for understanding unconventional transport in metals dominated by electron-electron scattering.

This study examines a two-orbital model of iron-based superconductors to understand how electron correlation and Jahn-Teller electron-phonon coupling interact. Using dynamical mean-field theory with exact diagonalization, we analyze the effects of increasing intra- and inter-orbital Coulomb interactions, \(U\) and \(U'\), under the condition \(U = U'\). Without Hund’s coupling \(J\) and electron-phonon coupling \(g\), both local spin susceptibility \(\chi_s\) and orbital susceptibility \(\chi_o\) increase equally, with \(\chi_s = \chi_o\).

When \(J\) and \(g\) are present, two distinct regimes emerge. For \(J \gtrsim 2g^2/\omega_0\) (where \(\omega_0\) is the phonon frequency), \(\chi_s\) is enhanced relative to \(\chi_o\) and diverges at a critical \(J_c\), beyond which the system transitions to a Mott insulator. Conversely, for \(J \lesssim 2g^2/\omega_0\), \(\chi_o\) is enhanced relative to \(\chi_s\) and diverges at a critical \(g_c\), leading to a bipolaronic insulator.

In the first regime, superconductivity is driven by antiferromagnetic fluctuations, which are amplified by Fermi-surface nesting, and its behavior strongly depends on carrier doping. In the second regime, superconductivity is mediated by ferro-orbital fluctuations and occurs over a broad doping range, including heavily doped cases without Fermi-surface nesting.

In a half-metallic ferromagnet with a short impurity mean free path, only spin-polarized Cooper pairs that are nonlocal and antisymmetrically correlated in time can survive. This causes the half-metal to behave as an odd-frequency superconducting condensate. Through analytical and numerical methods, we show that quantum vortices can form in these materials even in the absence of ordinary superconducting correlations. Since conduction occurs in only one spin band, a circulating spin supercurrent accompanies these vortices. We further find that magnetic disorder at the superconductor interfaces affects where vortices nucleate. Because vortex positions can be observed experimentally using STM measurements, this understanding can help determine effective interfacial magnetization misalignment angles in hybrid structures. Finally, we briefly discuss the appropriate superconducting order parameter for odd-frequency triplet Cooper pairs within quasiclassical theory.

We examine the possibility of directly detecting WIMPs arriving at Earth from beyond the Milky Way. Although their flux is far lower than that of typical galactic halo WIMPs, such extragalactic WIMPs could form a distinct population with several features that might help in their eventual detection. These include a high, nearly unique velocity—around 600 km/s in the galactic rest frame—resulting from acceleration as they enter the Milky Way, and likely one or two specific directions of arrival at Earth. This high velocity could be beneficial in direct detection experiments, as it produces nuclear recoils at relatively high energies where backgrounds are typically much lower. For an extragalactic WIMP density similar to the critical density of the universe, the expected event rate is roughly comparable to that of fast galactic WIMPs. If the density is enhanced relative to the critical density—for instance, by a factor of 10–30 due to the Local Group of galaxies—the rate would increase correspondingly, causing extragalactic WIMPs to appear as a high-energy shoulder in the recoil energy spectrum. Detecting such a separate population with these distinctive properties would provide valuable insights into the formation and early history of the galaxy.

This study introduces a model to capture the statistical fluctuations of self-driven agents (species A) moving against an opposing crowd (species B), simulating stop-and-go wave patterns in pedestrian dynamics. The approach employs single-biased random walks (SBRW), modeled through both partial differential equations and Monte-Carlo simulations. While the system remains non-interacting until opposite particles occupy the same cell, delays in particle residence times per cell then depend on the concentration of the opposing species. Analysis of particle position fluctuations reveals non-regular diffusion with long-tailed, asymmetric distributions, which align more closely with certain chromatograph distributions reported in the literature. Additionally, the presence of reverse crowd particles expands the dispersion of target particles relative to the non-biased case ($\alpha = 0$), following a brief initial reduction in dispersion.

In a regular graph, a regular clique is one where each vertex outside the clique is connected to an equal, positive number of vertices within the clique. Building on work started by A. Neumaier in the 1980s, which continues to draw attention, we examine regular cliques in edge-regular graphs. We introduce the term Neumaier graph to describe a non-complete edge-regular graph that includes a regular clique, and define a strictly Neumaier graph as one that is not strongly regular. We begin by establishing general properties of Neumaier graphs and their possible parameter sets. Using these findings, we identify the smallest strictly Neumaier graph, which contains 16 vertices. We then determine all parameter sets for strictly Neumaier graphs with up to 24 vertices. Lastly, we present two infinite families of graphs: for each positive integer i, the i-th graph in each sequence is a strictly Neumaier graph with a 2^i-regular clique and shares its edge-regular parameters with an affine polar graph. These results address recent questions raised by G. Greaves and J. Koolen.

Graph mining tasks like Clustering often require heavy computational resources.
